![9781484202005_Fig11-08.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-08.jpg)

[Figure 11-8](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig8). The Show Detail segue linking the master and detail view controllers

As you saw in [Chapter 9](#9781484202005_Ch09.xhtml), a segue thatÔÇÖs linked to a table view cell is triggered when that cell is selected, so when you select a row in the master view controllerÔÇÖs table view, iOS performs the Show Detail segue, with the navigation controller wrapping the detail view controller as the segue destination. This causes two things to happen:

*   A new instance of the detail view controller is created and its view is added to the view hierarchy.
*   The `prepareForSegue:sender:` method in the master view controller is called.

The first step takes care of making sure the detail view controller is visible. In the second step, your master view controller needs to display the object selected in the master view controller in some way. HereÔÇÖs how the template code in *MasterViewController.m* handles this:

```
- (void)prepareForSegue:(UIStoryboardSegue *)segue sender:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([[segue identifier] isEqualToString:@"showDetail"]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSIndexPath *indexPath =┬á[self.tableView indexPathForSelectedRow];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDate *object =┬áself.objects[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áDetailViewController *controller =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (DetailViewController *)[[segue destinationViewController] topViewController];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[controller setDetailItem:object];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =┬áself.splitViewController.┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádisplayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.navigationItem.leftItemsSupplementBackButton =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

First, the segue identifier is checked to make sure that itÔÇÖs the one that is expected and that the `NSDate` object from the selected object in the view controllerÔÇÖs table is obtained. Next, the master view controller finds the `DetailViewController` instance from the `topViewController` property of the destination view controller in the segue that caused this method to be called. Now that we have both the selected object and the detail view controller, all we have to do is set the detail view controllerÔÇÖs `detailItem` property to cause the detail view to be updated. The final two lines of the `prepareForSegue:sender:` method add the display mode button to the detail view controllerÔÇÖs navigation bar. When the device is in landscape mode, this doesnÔÇÖt do anything because the display mode button isnÔÇÖt visible, but if you rotate to portrait orientation, youÔÇÖll see that the button (itÔÇÖs the **Master** button) appears.

So now you know how the selected item in the master view controller gets displayed in the detail view controller. Although it doesnÔÇÖt look like much is going on here, in fact there is a great deal happening under the hood to make this work correctly on both the iPad and the iPhone, in portrait and landscape orientations. The beauty of the split view controller is that it takes care of all the details and leaves you free to worry about how to implement your custom master and detail view controllers.

That concludes the overview of what XcodeÔÇÖs Master-Detail Application template gives you. It might be a lot to absorb at a glance, but, ideally, presenting it a piece at a time has helped you understand how all the pieces fit together.

Here Come the Presidents

Now that youÔÇÖve seen the basic layout of our project, itÔÇÖs time to fill in the blanks and turn the template app into something all your own. Start by looking in the bookÔÇÖs source code archive, where the folder *11 ÔÇô Presidents Data* contains a file called *PresidentList.plist*. Drag that file into your projectÔÇÖs *Presidents* folder in Xcode to add it to the project, making sure that the check box telling Xcode to copy the file itself is checked. This *.plist* file contains information about all the US presidents so far, consisting of just the name and the Wikipedia entry URL for each of them.

Now, letÔÇÖs look at the master view controller and see how we need to modify it to handle the presidential data properly. ItÔÇÖs going to be a simple matter of loading the list of presidents, presenting them in the table view, and passing a URL to the detail view for display. In *MasterViewController.m*, start off by adding the bold line shown here to the class extension and removing the crossed-out line:

```
@interface MasterViewController ()

@property NSMutableArray *objects;
@property (copy, nonatomic) NSArray *presidents;

@end
```

Instead of holding our list of presidents in the mutable array that was created by Xcode, we create our own immutable array with a more meaningful name.

Now divert your attention to the `viewDidLoad` method, where the changes are a little more involved (but still not too bad). YouÔÇÖre going to add a few lines to load the list of presidents, and then remove a few other lines that set up edit and insertion buttons in the toolbar:

```
- (void)viewDidLoad {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewDidLoad];
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Do any additional setup after loading the view, typically from a┬ánib.
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =┬áself.editButtonItem;

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *path =┬á[[NSBundle mainBundle] pathForResource:@"PresidentList"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ofType:@"plist"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *presidentInfo =┬á[NSDictionary
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á dictionaryWithContentsOfFile:path];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.presidents =┬á[presidentInfo objectForKey:@"presidents"];

┬á┬á┬á┬áUIBarButtonItem *addButton =┬á[[UIBarButtonItem alloc]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á initWithBarButtonSystemItem:UIBarButtonSystemItemAdd target:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áaction:@selector(insertNewObject:)];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.navigationItem.rightBarButtonItem =┬áaddButton;
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.detailViewController =┬á(DetailViewController *)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [[self.splitViewController.viewControllers lastObject] topViewController];
}
```

This template-generated class also includes a method called `insertNewObject:` for adding items to the `objects` array. We donÔÇÖt even have that array anymore, so we delete the entire method:

```
- (void)insertNewObject:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!_objects) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_objects =┬á[[NSMutableArray alloc] init];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á[_objects insertObject:[NSDate date] atIndex:0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSIndexPath *indexPath =┬á[NSIndexPath indexPathForRow:0 inSection:0];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.tableView insertRowsAtIndexPaths:@[indexPath]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á withRowAnimation:UITableViewRowAnimationAutomatic];
}
```

Also, we have a couple of data source methods that deal with letting users edit rows in the table view. WeÔÇÖre not going to allow any editing of rows in this app, so letÔÇÖs just remove this code before adding our own:

```
- (BOOL)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á canEditRowAtIndexPath:(NSIndexPath *)indexPath {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Return NO if you do not want the specified item to be editable.
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
}

- (void)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácommitEditingStyle:(UITableViewCellEditingStyle)editingStyle
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áforRowAtIndexPath:(NSIndexPath *)indexPath {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (editingStyle == UITableViewCellEditingStyleDelete) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.objects removeObjectAtIndex:indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[tableView deleteRowsAtIndexPaths:@[indexPath]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á withRowAnimation:UITableViewRowAnimationFade];
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else if (editingStyle == UITableViewCellEditingStyleInsert) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Create a┬ánew instance of the appropriate class, insert it into the array,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á and add a┬ánew row to the table view.
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Now itÔÇÖs time to get to the main table view data source methods, adapting them for our purposes. LetÔÇÖs start by editing the method that tells the table view how many rows to display:

```
- (NSInteger)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ánumberOfRowsInSection:(NSInteger)section {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self.objects.count;
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [self.presidents count];
}
```

After that, edit the `tableView:cellForRowAtIndexPath:` method to make each cell display a presidentÔÇÖs name:

```
- (UITableViewCell *)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácellForRowAtIndexPath:(NSIndexPath *)indexPath {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUITableViewCell *cell =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [tableView dequeueReusableCellWithIdentifier:@"Cell" forIndexPath:indexPath];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDate *object =┬áself.objects[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬ácell.textLabel.text =┬á[object description];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *president =┬áself.presidents[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬ácell.textLabel.text =┬ápresident[@"name"];

┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn cell;
}
```

Finally, edit the `prepareForSegue:sender:` method to pass the data for the selected president (which is an `NSDictionary`) to the detail view controller, as follows:

```
- (void)prepareForSegue:(UIStoryboardSegue *)segue sender:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([[segue identifier] isEqualToString:@"showDetail"]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSIndexPath *indexPath =┬á[self.tableView indexPathForSelectedRow];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDate *object =┬áself.objects[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áDetailViewController *controller =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (DetailViewController *)[[segue destinationViewController] topViewController];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[controller setDetailItem:object];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *president =┬áself.presidents[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller .detailItem =┬ápresident;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á self.splitViewController.displayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.navigationItem.leftItemsSupplementBackButton =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

ThatÔÇÖs all we need to do in the master view controller.

Next, select *Main.storyboard* and click the **Master** icon in the **Master Scene** in the Document Outline to select the master view controller, and then double-click its title bar and replace *Master* with *Presidents* and save the storyboard.

At this point, you can build and run the app. Switch to landscape mode, or tap the **Master** button in the upper-left corner to bring up the master view controller, showing a list of presidents (see [Figure 11-9](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig9)). Tap a presidentÔÇÖs name to display a not-very-useful string in the detail view.

![9781484202005_Fig11-09.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-09.jpg)

[Figure 11-9](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig9). Our first run of the Presidents app, showing a list of presidents in the master view controller, but nothing useful in the detail view

LetÔÇÖs finish this example by making the detail view do something a little more useful with the data that itÔÇÖs given. Start with *DetailViewContoller.h*, where weÔÇÖll add an outlet for a web view to display the Wikipedia page for the selected president. Add the bold line shown here:

```
@interface DetailViewController :┬áUIViewController

@property (strong, nonatomic) id detailItem;
@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet UILabel *detailDescriptionLabel;
@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet UIWebView *webView;

@end
```

Next, switch to *DetailViewController.m*, where we have a bit more to do (though really, not too much). Scroll down to the `configureView` method and replace it with the following code:

```
- (void)configureView {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Update the user interface for the detail item.
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.detailItem) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *dict =┬á(NSDictionary *)self.detailItem;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *urlString =┬ádict[@"url"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.detailDescriptionLabel.text =┬áurlString;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *url =┬á[NSURL URLWithString:urlString];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURLRequest *request =┬á[NSURLRequest requestWithURL:url];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.webView loadRequest:request];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *name =┬ádict[@"name"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.title =┬áname;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

The `detailItem` that was set by the master view controller is an `NSDictionary` containing two key-value pairs: one with a key `name` that stores the presidentÔÇÖs name and another with a key `url` that gives the URL of the presidentÔÇÖs Wikipedia page. We use the URL to set the text of the detail description label and to construct an `NSURLRequest` that the `UIWebView` will use to load the page. We use the name to set the detail view controllerÔÇÖs title. When a view controller is a container in a `UINavigationController`, the value in its `title` property is displayed in the navigation controllerÔÇÖs navigation bar. ThatÔÇÖs all we need to get our web view to load the requested page.

The final changes we need to make are in *Main.storyboard*. Open it for editing and find the detail view at the lower right. LetÔÇÖs first take care of the label in the GUI (the text of which reads, ÔÇ£Detail view content goes hereÔÇØ).

Start by selecting the label. You might find it easiest to select the label in the Document Outline, in the section labeled *Detail Scene*. Once the label is selected, drag it to the top of the window. The label should run from the left-to-right blue guideline and fit snugly under the navigation bar (resize it to make sure that is the case). This label is being repurposed to show the current URL. But when the application launches, before the user has chosen a president, we want this field to give the user a hint about what to do.

Double-click the label and change it to *Select a President*. You should also use the Size Inspector to make sure that the labelÔÇÖs position is constrained to both the left and right sides of its superview, as well as the top edge (see [Figure 11-10](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig10)). If you need to adjust these constraints, use the methods described earlier to set them up. You can probably get almost exactly what you want by selecting the label and then choosing **Editor ![image](images/arrow.jpg) Resolve Auto Layout Issues ![image](images/arrow.jpg) Reset to Suggested Constraints** from the menu.

![9781484202005_Fig11-10.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-10.jpg)

[Figure 11-10](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig10). The Size Inspector, showing the constraints settings for the ÔÇ£Select a PresidentÔÇØ label at the bottom

Next, use the library to find a `UIWebView` and drag it into the space below the label you just moved. After dropping the web view there, use the resize handles to make it fill the rest of the view below the label. Make it go from the left edge to the right edge, and from the blue guideline just below the bottom of the label all the way to the very bottom of the window. Now use the Size Inspector to constrain the web view to the left, bottom, and right edges of the superview, as well as to the label for the top edge (see [Figure 11-11](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig11)). Once again, you can probably get exactly what you need by selecting **Editor ![image](images/arrow.jpg) Resolve Auto Layout Issues ![image](images/arrow.jpg) Reset to Suggested Constraints** from the menu.

![9781484202005_Fig11-11.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-11.jpg)

[Figure 11-11](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig11). The Size Inspector, showing the constraints settings for the web view

Now select the **Master** view controller in the Document Outline and open the Attributes Editor. In the **View Controller** section, change the **Title** from *Master* to *Presidents*. This changes the title of the navigation button at the top of the detail view controller to something more useful.

We have one last step to complete. To hook up the outlet for the web view that you created, Control-drag from the **Detail** icon (in the Detail Scene section in the Document Outline) to our new Web View (same section, just below the label in the Document Outline, or in the storyboard), and connect the `webView` outlet. Save your changes, and youÔÇÖre finished!

Now you can build and run the app, and it will let you see the Wikipedia entries for each of the presidents (see [Figure 11-12](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig12)). Rotate the display between the two orientations, and youÔÇÖll see how the split view controller takes care of everything for you, with a little help from the detail view controller.

![9781484202005_Fig11-12.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-12.jpg)

[Figure 11-12](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig12). The Presidents application, showing the Wikipedia page for George Washington

Creating Your Own Popover

Back in [Chapter 4](#9781484202005_Ch04.xhtml), you saw that you can display an action sheet in what looks like a cartoon speech bubble (see [Figure 4-28](#9781484202005_Ch04.xhtml#Fig28)). That speech bubble is the visual representation of a **popover controller**, or *popover* for short. The popover that you get with an action sheet is created for you when the action sheet is presented by a `UIPopoverPresentationController`, which you have very little control over. However, you can create your own popover by using the `UIPopoverController` class, which can come in handy when you want to present your own view controllers.

To see how this works, weÔÇÖre going to add a popover to be activated by a permanent toolbar item (unlike the one in the `UISplitView`, which is meant to come and go). This popover will display a table view containing a list of languages. If the user picks a language from the list, the web view will load (in the new language) whatever Wikipedia entry that was already showing. This will be simple enough to do, since switching from one language to another in Wikipedia is just a matter of changing a small piece of the URL that contains an embedded country code. [Figure 11-3](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig3) shows what we are aiming for. ItÔÇÖs important to note, however, that `UIPopoverController` is available only on the iPad, so when this application is run on the iPhone, the language selector will be missing.

**Note**┬á┬áThe use of a popover in this example is in the service of showing a `UITableView`, but donÔÇÖt let that mislead youÔÇö`UIPopoverController` can be used to handle the display of any view controller content you like! WeÔÇÖre sticking with table views for this example because itÔÇÖs a common use case, itÔÇÖs easy to show in a relatively small amount of code, and itÔÇÖs something with which you should already be quite familiar.

Start by right-clicking the *Presidents* folder in Xcode and selecting **New File. . .** from the pop-up menu. When the assistant appears, select **Cocoa Touch Class** from the **iOS Source** section, and then click **Next**. On the next screen, name the new class *LanguageListController* and select `UITableViewController` from the **Subclass of** field. Click **Next**, double-check the location where youÔÇÖre saving the file, and click **Create**.

The `LanguageListController` is going to be a pretty standard table view controller class. It will display a list of items and let the detail view controller know when a choice is made, by using a pointer back to the detail view controller. Edit *LanguageListController.h*, adding the bold lines shown here:

```
#import <UIKit/UIKit.h>

@class DetailViewController;

@interface LanguageListController :┬áUITableViewController

@property (weak, nonatomic) DetailViewController *detailViewController;
@property (copy, nonatomic) NSArray *languageNames;
@property (copy, nonatomic) NSArray *languageCodes;

@end
```

These additions define a pointer back to the detail view controller (which weÔÇÖll set from code in the detail view controller itself when weÔÇÖre about to display the language list), as well as a pair of arrays for containing the values that will be displayed (English, French, etc.) and the underlying values that will be used to build an URL from the chosen language (`en`, `fr`, and so on). Note that weÔÇÖve declared these arrays to have `copy` storage semantics instead of `strong`. This means that whenever some piece of code calls one of these setters, the parameter is sent a `copy` message instead of just being held as a `strong` pointer. This is done to prevent a situation where another class might send in an `NSMutableArray` instead of an `NSArray`, and then make changes to the array without our knowledge. Sending `copy` to an `NSMutableArray` instance always returns an immutable `NSArray`, so we know that the array weÔÇÖre using canÔÇÖt be changed by someone else. At the same time, sending `copy` to an `NSArray`, which is already immutable, doesnÔÇÖt actually make a new copy, it just returns a strong pointer to `self`, so sending it a `copy` message isnÔÇÖt wasteful in any way.

If you copied and pasted this code from the bookÔÇÖs source archive (or e-book) into your own project or typed it yourself a little sloppily, you may not have noticed an important difference in how the `detailViewController` property was declared earlier. Unlike most properties that reference an object pointer, we declared this one using `weak` instead of `strong`. This is something that we must do to avoid a **retain** cycle.

WhatÔÇÖs a retain cycle? ItÔÇÖs a situation where a set of two or more objects have references to each other, in a circular fashion. Each object is keeping the memory of the other object from being freed. Most potential retain cycles can be avoided by carefully considering the creation of your objects, often by trying to figure out which object ÔÇ£ownsÔÇØ which. In this sense, an instance of `DetailViewController` owns an instance of `LanguageListController` because itÔÇÖs the `DetailViewController` that actually creates the `LanguageListController` to get a piece of work done. Whenever you have a pair of objects that need to refer to one another, youÔÇÖll usually want the owner object to retain the other object, while the other object should specifically not retain its owner. Since weÔÇÖre using the ARC feature that Apple introduced in Xcode 4.2, the compiler does most of the work for us. Instead of paying attention to the details about releasing and retaining objects, all we need to do is declare a property that refers to an object that we do not own with the `weak` keyword instead of `strong`. ARC will do the rest!

Now, switch over to *LanguageListController.m* to implement the following changes. At the top of the file, start by importing the header for `DetailViewController`:

```
#import "LanguageListController.h"
#import "DetailViewController.h"
.
.
.
```

Next, scroll down a bit to the `viewDidLoad` method and add a bit of setup code:

```
- (void)viewDidLoad {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewDidLoad];

┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languageNames =┬á@[@"English", @"French", @"German", @"Spanish"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languageCodes =┬á@[@"en", @"fr", @"de", @"es"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.clearsSelectionOnViewWillAppear =┬áNO;
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.preferredContentSize =┬áCGSizeMake(320.0,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [self.languageCodes count] *┬á44.0);

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.tableView registerClass:[UITableViewCell class]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á forCellReuseIdentifier:@"Cell"];

}
```

This sets up the language arrays and also defines the size that the view controllerÔÇÖs view will use if shown in a popover (which, as we know, it will be). Without defining the size, we would end up with a popover stretching vertically to fill nearly the whole screen, even if it can be displayed in full with a much smaller view. And finally, we register a default table view cell class to use, as explained in [Chapter 8](#9781484202005_Ch08.xhtml).

Further down, we have a few methods generated by XcodeÔÇÖs template that donÔÇÖt contain particularly useful codeÔÇöjust a warning and some placeholder text. LetÔÇÖs replace those with something real:

```
- (NSInteger)numberOfSectionsInTableView:(UITableView *)tableView {
#warning Potentially incomplete method implementation.
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn 0;
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn 1;
}

- (NSInteger)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬ánumberOfRowsInSection:(NSInteger)section {
#warning Incomplete method implementation.
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Return the number of rows in the section.
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn 0;
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [self.languageCodes count];
}
```

Now add the `tableView:cellForRowAtIndexPath:` method to get a cell object and put a language name into a cell:

```
- (UITableViewCell *)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á cellForRowAtIndexPath:(NSIndexPath *)indexPath {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUITableViewCell *cell =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[tableView dequeueReusableCellWithIdentifier:@"Cell" forIndexPath:indexPath];

┬á┬á┬á┬á// Configure the cell...
┬á┬á┬á┬ácell.textLabel.text =┬áself.languageNames[indexPath.row];

┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn cell;
}
```

Next, implement `tableView:didSelectRowAtIndexPath:` so that you can respond to a userÔÇÖs touch by passing the language selection back to the detail view controller:

```
- (void)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádidSelectRowAtIndexPath:(NSIndexPath *)indexPath {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.detailViewController.languageString =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languageCodes[indexPath.row];
}
```

**Note**┬á┬á`DetailViewController` doesnÔÇÖt actually have a `languageString` property yet, so you will see a compiler error. WeÔÇÖll take care of that in just a bit.

Now itÔÇÖs time to make the changes required for `DetailViewController` to handle the popover, as well as to generate the correct URL whenever the user either changes the display language or picks a different president. Start by making the following changes in *DetailViewController.h*:

```
#import <UIKit/UIKit.h>

@interface DetailViewController :┬áUIViewController

@property (strong, nonatomic) id detailItem;
@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet UILabel *detailDescriptionLabel;
@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet UIWebView *webView;
@property (strong, nonatomic) UIBarButtonItem *languageButton;
@property (strong, nonatomic) UIPopoverController *languagePopoverController;
@property (copy, nonatomic) NSString *languageString;

@end
```

Here, we added some properties to keep track of the GUI components required for the popover and the userÔÇÖs selected language. All we need to do now is fix *DetailViewController.m* so that it can handle the language popover and the URL construction. Start by adding this import somewhere at the top and conforming the class to the `UIPopoverControllerDelegate` protocol so that it can respond to messages from the `UIPopoverController`:

```
#import "DetailViewController.h"
#import "LanguageListController.h"

@interface DetailViewController () <UIPopoverControllerDelegate>

@end
```

The next thing weÔÇÖre going to add is a function that takes as arguments a URL pointing to a Wikipedia page and a two-letter language code, and then returns a URL that combines the two. WeÔÇÖll use this at appropriate spots in our controller code later. You can place this function just about anywhere, including within the classÔÇÖs implementation. The compiler is smart enough to always treat a function as just a function. Place it just after the `setDetailItem:` method:

```
static NSString *┬ámodifyUrlForLanguage(NSString *url, NSString *lang) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!lang) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn url;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}

┬á┬á┬á┬á// We're relying on a┬áparticular Wikipedia URL format here. This
┬á┬á┬á┬á// is a┬ábit fragile!
┬á┬á┬á┬á// URL is like http://en.wikipedia...
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSRange codeRange =┬áNSMakeRange(7, 2);
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([[url substringWithRange:codeRange] isEqualToString:lang]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn url;
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *newUrl =┬á[url stringByReplacingCharactersInRange:codeRange
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áwithString:lang];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn newUrl;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Why make this a function instead of a method? There are a couple of reasons. First, instance methods in a class are typically meant to do something involving one or more instance variables, or accessing an objectÔÇÖs internal state either through getters and setters or through direct instance variable access. This function does not use any instance variables. It simply performs an operation on two strings and returns another. We could have made it a class method, but even that feels a bit wrong, since what the method does isnÔÇÖt really related specifically to the controller class. Sometimes, a function is just what you need.

Our next move is to update the `configureView:` method. This method will use the function we just defined to combine the URL thatÔÇÖs passed in with the chosen `languageString` to generate the correct URL:

```
- (void)configureView {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Update the user interface for the detail item.
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.detailItem) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *dict =┬á(NSDictionary *)self.detailItem;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *urlString =┬ámodifyUrlForLanguage(dict[@"url"], self.languageString);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.detailDescriptionLabel.text =┬áurlString;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *url =┬á[NSURL URLWithString:urlString];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURLRequest *request =┬á[NSURLRequest requestWithURL:url];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.webView loadRequest:request];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *name =┬ádict[@"name"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.title =┬áname;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Now letÔÇÖs update the `viewDidLoad` method. Here, weÔÇÖre going to create a `UIBarButtonItem` and put it into the `UINavigationItem` at the top of the screen, but only if we are running on an iPad:

```
- (void)viewDidLoad {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewDidLoad];

┬á┬á┬á┬áif (UI_USER_INTERFACE_IDIOM() == UIUserInterfaceIdiomPad) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languageButton =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [[UIBarButtonItem alloc] initWithTitle:@"Choose Language"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ástyle:UIBarButtonItemStylePlain
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á target:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á action:@selector(toggleLanguagePopover)];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.navigationItem.rightBarButtonItem =┬áself.languageButton;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self configureView];
}
```

YouÔÇÖll get a compiler warning here because the code you just added refers to a method called `toggleLanguagePopover` that doesnÔÇÖt exist. WeÔÇÖll fix that soon. Here, we use `UI_USER_INTERFACE_IDIOM()` to determine whether weÔÇÖre running on an iPad or an iPhone. We only want to add the button on an iPad, because iPhones do not support `UIPopoverController`.

Next, we implement `setLanguageString:`, which is called when the value of the `languageString` property is changed. This property setter method calls `configureView` so that the URL can be regenerated (and the new page loaded) immediately, and dismisses the language selection popover if itÔÇÖs visible. Add this method to the bottom of the file, just above the `@end`:

```
- (void)setLanguageString:(NSString *)newString {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (![newString isEqualToString:self.languageString]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_languageString =┬á[newString copy];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self configureView];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.languagePopoverController != nil) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.languagePopoverController dismissPopoverAnimated:YES];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languagePopoverController =┬ánil;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Now, letÔÇÖs define what will happen when the user taps the **Choose Language** button. Simply put, we create a `LanguageListController`, wrap it in a `UIPopoverController`, and display it. Place this method after the `viewDidLoad` method:

```
- (void)toggleLanguagePopover {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.languagePopoverController == nil) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áLanguageListController *languageListController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[[LanguageListController alloc] init];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álanguageListController.detailViewController =┬áself;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áUIPopoverController *poc =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[[UIPopoverController alloc]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á initWithContentViewController:languageListController];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[poc presentPopoverFromBarButtonItem:self.languageButton
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ápermittedArrowDirections:UIPopoverArrowDirectionAny
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áanimated:YES];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languagePopoverController =┬ápoc;
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.languagePopoverController dismissPopoverAnimated:YES];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languagePopoverController =┬ánil;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Finally, we need to implement one more method to handle the situation where the user taps to open our Languages popover, and then taps somewhere outside the popover to make it go away. In that case, our `toggleLanguagePopover` method isnÔÇÖt called. However, we can implement a method declared in the `UIPopoverControllerDelegate` protocol to be notified when that happens, and then remove the language Popovers:

```
- (void)popoverControllerDidDismissPopovers:
┬á┬á┬á┬á(UIPopoverController *)popoverController┬á┬á{
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (popoverController == self.languagePopoverController) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.languagePopoverController =┬ánil;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

And thatÔÇÖs all! You should now be able to run the app in all its glory, switching willy-nilly between presidents and languages. Switching from one language to another should always leave the chosen president intact. Likewise, switching from one president to another should leave the language intactÔÇöbut actually, it doesnÔÇÖt. Try this: choose a president, change the language to (say) Spanish, and then choose another president. Unfortunately, the language is no longer Spanish.

Why did this happen? If you go back to ÔÇ£How the Master-Detail Template Application WorksÔÇØ section, youÔÇÖll discover the problemÔÇöthe Show Detail segue creates a new instance of the detail view controller every time itÔÇÖs performed. That means that the language setting, which is stored as a property of the detail view controller, is going to be lost each time a new president is selected. To fix it, we need to add a few lines of code in the master view controller. Open *MasterViewController.m* and make the following changes to the `prepareForSegue:sender:` method:

```
- (void)prepareForSegue:(UIStoryboardSegue *)segue sender:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([[segue identifier] isEqualToString:@"showDetail"]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSIndexPath *indexPath =┬á[self.tableView indexPathForSelectedRow];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áDetailViewController *controller =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (DetailViewController *)[[segue destinationViewController] topViewController];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.languageString =┬áself.detailViewController.languageString;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.detailViewController =┬ácontroller;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *president =┬áself.presidents[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.detailItem =┬ápresident;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =┬áself.splitViewController.
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádisplayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontroller.navigationItem.leftItemsSupplementBackButton =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Recall that we saved a reference to the detail view controller in the `detailViewController` property in the master view controllerÔÇÖs `viewDidLoad` method. Here, when we are about to perform the segue, we use that reference to get the value of the `languageString` property from the old instance of the detail view controller and copy it to the new instance, which has already replaced the old one in the split view controllerÔÇÖs view hierarchy. Then, we update the `detailViewController` property of the new instance. ThatÔÇÖs all we need to do. Now run the application again. YouÔÇÖll find that you can switch between presidents without losing your chosen language.

Split Views on the iPhone

As of iOS 8, the split view controller is available on the iPhone as well as the iPad. However, the smaller screen size of the iPhone means that the split view controller works slightly differently than it does on the iPad. Select the iPhone 5s simulator and run the Presidents app in portrait mode. YouÔÇÖll see the difference immediately (see [Figure 11-13](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig13)): the list of presidents in the master view controller is visible, but the detail view controllerÔÇÖs view is missing. Rotate the device to landscape, and youÔÇÖll see that you can still see only the master view controller.

![9781484202005_Fig11-13.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-13.jpg)

[Figure 11-13](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig13). The Presidents app running on an iPhone 5s

To activate the detail view controller, just select a president. The detail view controllerÔÇÖs view slides in from the right and the **Presidents** button appears at the top of the navigation bar, as shown in [Figure 11-14](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig14). Notice also that the **Choose Language** button is missing, because we donÔÇÖt create one when running on an iPhone. If you press the **Back** button, the detail view controllerÔÇÖs view slides out to the right and the list of presidents reappears.

![9781484202005_Fig11-14.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-14.jpg)

[Figure 11-14](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig14). The Presidents appÔÇÖs detail view controller on iPhone

ItÔÇÖs important to note that we havenÔÇÖt had to change any code to make the application work on the iPhone. The split view controller sets itself up differently in the constrained screen space of the iPhone, initially showing only the master view controller. In this mode, the split view controller is said to be **collapsed**. In collapsed mode, the Set Detail segue that we used to link the master view controller to the detail view controller behaves differentlyÔÇöinstead of displaying the detail view controller in its own dedicated space on the screen, the split view pushes it onto the view controller stack of the master view controllerÔÇÖs `UINavigationController`. When you press the **Presidents** button to redisplay the presidents list, the detail view controller is popped off the stack, exposing the table view controller that was underneath it.

Split Views on the iPhone 6 Plus

The behavior you have just seen applies to all iPhones, with the exception of the iPhone 6 Plus. In landscape mode, the iPhone 6 Plus has a large enough screen to permit the split view to show both view controllers side by side, as it does on the iPad, but only when in landscape mode. Run the application on the iPhone 6 Plus simulator. YouÔÇÖll initially see just the master view controller, as usual. Now rotate to landscape mode, and youÔÇÖll see both view controllers ([Figure 11-15](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig15)).

![9781484202005_Fig11-15.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-15.jpg)

[Figure 11-15](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig15). The PresidentÔÇÖs app in landscape mode on the iPhone 6 Plus

This is similar to, but not exactly the same as, on the iPad. If you compare [Figure 11-15](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig15) with [Figure 11-3](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig3), youÔÇÖll see that the iPhone version has an extra, double-headed button at the top left of the detail view controllerÔÇÖs navigation bar. This is actually the **Presidents** button (the one obtained from the `displayModeButtonItem` property of the `UISplitViewController` in the application delegate), drawn differently to reflect its modified function. If you press this button, the master view controller is removed, leaving the detail view controller with the whole screen, and the button reverts to its normal appearance. Press the button again to bring the master view controller back into view.

The difference in behavior of the iPhone 6 Plus is another feature that you get for free from `UISplitViewController`. There are various ways to customize the behavior of the split view, usually by implementing various methods of the `UISplitViewDelegate` protocol. WeÔÇÖre not going to say anything more about that here, except to point out one detail that youÔÇÖll observe if you restart the application and turn the simulator to portrait mode. At this point, as always, youÔÇÖll see the master view controller. If you switch between portrait and landscape modes, youÔÇÖll continue to see the same controller. Now rotate to landscape mode and select a president, and then rotate back to portrait mode. This time, the detail view controller remains visibleÔÇöthe split view controller did not switch back to the master view controller. This behavior is the result of a `UISplitViewDelegate` method thatÔÇÖs implemented in *AppDelegate.m* and is the reason why the application delegate is registered as the split viewÔÇÖs delegate when the application launches. HereÔÇÖs how that method is implemented:

```
- (BOOL)splitViewController:(UISplitViewController *)splitViewController
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á collapseSecondaryViewController:(UIViewController *)secondaryViewController
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ontoPrimaryViewController:(UIViewController *)primaryViewController {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([secondaryViewController isKindOfClass:[UINavigationController class]]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á && [[(UINavigationController *)secondaryViewController topViewController]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á isKindOfClass:[DetailViewController class]]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á&& ([(DetailViewController *)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[(UINavigationController *)secondaryViewController topViewController]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádetailItem] == nil)) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Return YES to indicate that we have handled the collapse by doing nothing;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áthe secondary controller will be discarded.
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn NO; 
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

This method is called when the split view controller is switching between expanded mode (when both view controllers are active) and collapsed mode (when there is only one). If it returns `YES`, the detail view controller will be removed; but if it returns `NO`, the detail view controller will stay in view. The code that the Xcode template produces ensures that the detail view controller is not removed if its `detailItem` property is not `nil`ÔÇöthat is, if it is currently displaying something. Interestingly, if you stub out this method so that it always returns `NO`, youÔÇÖll find that the split view controller opens with the detail view controller visible instead of the master view controller.

Getting the iPhone 6 Plus Behavior on All iPhones

ItÔÇÖs possible to get the split view to behave as it does on the iPhone 6 Plus on all iPhones. To see how this is possible, you have to understand why the split view shows both view controllers in landscape mode on the iPhone 6 Plus but not on any other iPhone models. They key to this is a concept that was introduced back in Chapter 5ÔÇösize classes. If you look at [Figure 5-20](#9781484202005_Ch05.xhtml#Fig20), youÔÇÖll see that the horizontal size class for all iPhones in all orientations is Compact, apart from the iPhone 6 Plus, which has Regular size class in landscape mode. The split view controller operates in collapsed mode in a horizontally compact environment and in expanded mode otherwise. ThatÔÇÖs why you can see both view controllers on the iPhone 6 Plus when itÔÇÖs in landscape orientation. As it turns out, you can use this fact to get the same behavior on all iPhones. All you have to do is convince the split view controller that its horizontal size class is Regular.

Size class information is propagated to a view controller from its parent view controller, if it has one, or from its window if itÔÇÖs the top-level view controller. The size class information is delivered as part of a **trait** **collection**, represented by the `UITraitCollection` class, when the view controllerÔÇÖs view is displayed and when the device is rotated, if that would cause either its horizontal or vertical size class to change. All view controllers conform to the `UITraitEnvironment` protocol, which means that they have a `traitCollection` property that holds the current set of traits and they implement the following method, which is called after the value of the `traitCollection` property has been changed:

```
- (void)traitCollectionDidChange:(UITraitCollection *)previousTraitCollection
```

To make the split view controller believe that it has a Regular horizontal size class, we need to change the trait collection thatÔÇÖs passed to it by its parent view controller. That brings up a problem: in the storyboard thatÔÇÖs created by the Master-Detail application template (see [Figure 11-6](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig6)), the split view controller is the root view controller of its window, so it doesnÔÇÖt have a parent view controller. To change its trait collection, weÔÇÖll first have to give it a parent view controller, so letÔÇÖs do that.

Select *Main.storyboard*, open the Object Library, and drag a `UINavigationController` onto the storyboard. WeÔÇÖre going to make this controller the parent of the split view controller. It already has a `UITableViewController` child, which we donÔÇÖt need, so select it (itÔÇÖs the one labeled **Root View Controller** in the Document Outline) and delete it. Next, Control-drag from the navigation controller to the split view controller, and then release the mouse. In the pop-up, select **Root View Controller** from the **Relationship Segue** section to make the navigation controller the parent of the `UISplitViewController`. There are two final steps to complete. Select the navigation controller and open the Attributes Inspector. In the **Navigation Controller** section, uncheck **Shows Navigation Bar** (since we donÔÇÖt need to be able to navigate) and in the **View Controller** section, check **Is Initial View Controller** to make the navigation controller the root view controller of the application window.

**Note**┬á┬áIf you are wondering why we used a `UINavigationController` as the root view controller instead of a plain `UIViewController`, the reason is that Interface Builder wonÔÇÖt let you drag to create a controller connection from an ordinary `UIViewController`, because there is no `rootViewController` property. You could create the connection in code (as we did in [Chapter 6](#9781484202005_Ch06.xhtml)), but itÔÇÖs easier to just use a `UINavigationController` and switch off the navigation bar.

At this point, your storyboard should look something like [Figure 11-16](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig16).

![9781484202005_Fig11-16.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-16.jpg)

[Figure 11-16](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig16). The storyboard of the Presidents app with a new root view controller

Now that weÔÇÖve given the split view a parent view controller, we need to override its `traitCollectionDidChange:` method. To do that, we need to substitute our own `UINavigationController` subclass for the one in the storyboard. Right-click the *Presidents* folder in the Project Navigator and select **New File. . .**, and then choose **Cocoa Touch Class** from the **iOS Source** section of the new file chooser and click **Next**. Give the new class the name `RootViewController`, make it a subclass of `UINavigationController`, and create it. Select the navigation controller in *Main.storyboard*, open the Identity Inspector, and set its **Class** to `RootViewController`.

So now our navigation controller subclass is the windowÔÇÖs root view controller and we are almost ready to override its `traitCollectionDidChange:` method, but we have one more thing to fix before we do that. The template-generated code in `application:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:` in *AppDelegate.m* assumes that the split view controller is the root view controller. Since thatÔÇÖs no longer the case, we have to make a small change. Open *AppDelegate.m* and make the following changes shown in bold:

```
- (BOOL)application:(UIApplication *)application
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:(NSDictionary *)launchOptions {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Override point for customization after application launch.
┬á┬á┬á┬áUISplitViewController *splitViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (UISplitViewController *)self.window.rootViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *rootViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (UINavigationController *)self.window.rootViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áUISplitViewController *splitViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (UISplitViewController *)rootViewController.viewControllers[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *navigationController =┬á[splitViewController.viewControllers lastObject];
┬á┬á┬á┬ánavigationController.topViewController.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á splitViewController.displayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.delegate =┬áself;
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
}
```

Now letÔÇÖs do what we set out to do. Open *RootViewController.m* in the editor and add the following code to it:

```
- (void)traitCollectionDidChange:(UITraitCollection *)previousTraitCollection {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIViewController *spltVC =┬áself.viewControllers[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUITraitCollection *newTraits =┬áself.traitCollection;
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (newTraits.horizontalSizeClass == UIUserInterfaceSizeClassCompact
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á&& newTraits.verticalSizeClass == UIUserInterfaceSizeClassCompact) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áUITraitCollection *childTraits =┬á[UITraitCollection
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átraitCollectionWithHorizontalSizeClass:UIUserInterfaceSizeClassRegular];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setOverrideTraitCollection:childTraits forChildViewController:spltVC];
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setOverrideTraitCollection:nil forChildViewController:spltVC];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super traitCollectionDidChange:previousTraitCollection];
}
```

The first thing we do is get the newly installed set of traits from the root view controllerÔÇÖs `traitCollection` property. If both the horizontal and vertical size classes are Compact, then we must be running on an iPhone thatÔÇÖs been rotated to landscape. This is the case in which we need to change the horizontal size class that the split view will see from Compact to Regular. We do that by creating a trait for the regular size class using a class method of `UITraitCollection`:

```
UITraitCollection *childTraits =┬á[UITraitCollection
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átraitCollectionWithHorizontalSizeClass:UIUserInterfaceSizeClassRegular];
```

Next, we tell the root view controller to override the traits of its split view controller child with this new trait:

```
[self setOverrideTraitCollection:childTraits forChildViewController:spltVC];
```

On the other hand, if we have any other combination of size classes, we donÔÇÖt need to change them, so we install a `nil` override:

```
[self setOverrideTraitCollection:nil forChildViewController:spltVC];
```

Now build and run the application, and then run it on any iPhone simulator. Rotate to landscape, and youÔÇÖll see both view controllers, just like you would on the iPhone 6 Plus.

Incidentally, you can even force the split view controller to show both view controllers in portrait mode by modifying the `traitCollectionDidChange:` method so that it always installs an override trait. ItÔÇÖs worth trying that just to see that it works, but the screen is too narrow for this to be useful in most cases.

Customizing the Split View

There are a couple of split view controller customizations available that are worth experimenting with. These work on any device. First, you can control the width of the area allocated to the master view controller when both view controllers are visible. To do this, you need to set the split view controllerÔÇÖs `preferredPrimaryColumnWidthFraction` and `maximumPrimaryColumnWidth` properties. The former sets the width of the master view controller as a fraction of the total space available and requires a value between 0 and 1\. The latter acts as an upper bound on its width, so you need to set this property if you need the master view controller to be wider than the default value calculated by the split view controller.

To see how this works, make the following changes to `application:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:` in *AppDelegate.m*:

```
- (BOOL)application:(UIApplication *)application
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádidFinishLaunchingWithOptions:(NSDictionary *)launchOptions {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Override point for customization after application launch.
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *rootViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(UINavigationController *)self.window.rootViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áUISplitViewController *splitViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(UISplitViewController *)rootViewController.viewControllers[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *navigationController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[splitViewController.viewControllers lastObject];
┬á┬á┬á┬ánavigationController.topViewController.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.displayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.delegate =┬áself;

┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.preferredPrimaryColumnWidthFraction =┬á0.5;
┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.maximumPrimaryColumnWidth =┬á600;

┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
}
```

Run the application again on any simulator and rotate to landscape mode. YouÔÇÖll see that the master view controller now occupies half of the screen (see [Figure 11-17](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#Fig17)), because we set `preferredPrimaryColumnWidthFraction` to 0.5 and increased `maximumPrimaryColumnWidth` to a value thatÔÇÖs large enough that it doesnÔÇÖt limit the master view controllerÔÇÖs width on any current device.

![9781484202005_Fig11-17.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig11-17.jpg)

[Figure 11-17](#9781484202005_Ch11.xhtml#_Fig17). Increasing the width of the master view controller in a split view

The second customization controls the way in which the master view controller is managed. By default, the split view controller determines when this controller is visible and how it appears and disappears. For example, on the iPad in portrait mode, the master view controller is initially invisible and slides in from the left of the screen; whereas in landscape mode, itÔÇÖs initially visible and cannot be hidden. This behavior is controlled by the split view controllerÔÇÖs `preferredDisplayMode` property. By default, this is set to `UISplitViewControllerDisplayModeAutomatic`, but there are three other choices available:

*   `UISplitViewControllerDisplayModePrimaryOverlay`: Places the master view controller on the left, overlaying the detail view controller. When the master view controller is dismissed, it slides away to the left.
*   `UISplitViewControllerDisplayModePrimaryHidden`: The same as `UISplitViewControllerDisplayModePrimaryOverlay`, except that the master view controller is initially hidden.
*   `UISplitViewControllerDisplayModeAllVisible`: Makes both view controllers initially visible on the screen.

The actual behavior depends on the type of device. For example, in horizontal Compact mode, this property has no effect, since both view controllers are never on the screen at the same time.

You can try out each of these modes by setting the `preferredDisplayMode` property in the `application:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:` method. For example:

```
- (BOOL)application:(UIApplication *)application
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádidFinishLaunchingWithOptions:(NSDictionary *)launchOptions {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Override point for customization after application launch.
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *rootViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(UINavigationController *)self.window.rootViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áUISplitViewController *splitViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(UISplitViewController *)rootViewController.viewControllers[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *navigationController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[splitViewController.viewControllers lastObject];
┬á┬á┬á┬ánavigationController.topViewController.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.displayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.delegate =┬áself;

┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.preferredPrimaryColumnWidthFraction =┬á0.5;
┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.maximumPrimaryColumnWidth =┬á600;

┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.preferredDisplayMode =┬áUISplitViewControllerDisplayModePrimaryOverlay;

┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
}
```

Time to Wrap Up and Split

In this chapter, you learned about the split view controller and its role in the creation of Master-Detail applications. You also saw that a complex application with several interconnected view controllers can be configured entirely within Interface Builder. Although split views are now available on all devices, they are probably still most useful in the larger screen space of the iPhone 6 Plus and the iPad. If you want to dig even further into the particulars of iPad development, you may want to take a look at *Beginning iPad Development for iPhone Developers* by David Mark, Jack Nutting, and Dave Wooldridge (Apress, 2010).

Next up, itÔÇÖs time to visit application settings and user defaults.

Chapter┬á┬á┬á┬á12

Application Settings and User Defaults

All but the simplest computer programs today have a preferences window where the user can set application-specific options. On Mac OS X, the **PreferencesÔÇª** menu item is usually found in the application menu. Selecting it brings up a window where the user can enter and change various options. The iPhone and other iOS devices have a dedicated application called Settings, which you no doubt have played with any number of times. In this chapter, weÔÇÖll show you how to add settings for your application to the Settings application and how to access those settings from within your application.

Getting to Know Your Settings Bundle

The Settings application lets the user enter and change preferences for any application that has a settings bundle. A **settings bundle** is a group of files built in to an application that tells the Settings application which preferences the application wishes to collect from the user.

Pick up your iOS device and locate your Settings icon. Touch the icon to launch the Settings app. Ours is shown in [Figure 12-1](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig1)

![9781484202005_Fig12-01.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-01.jpg)

[Figure 12-1](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig1). The Settings application

.

The Settings application acts as a common user interface for the iOS User Defaults mechanism. User Defaults is the part of the system that stores and retrieves preferences.

In an iOS application, User Defaults is implemented by the `NSUserDefaults` class. If youÔÇÖve done Cocoa programming on the Mac, youÔÇÖre probably already familiar with `NSUserDefaults` because it is the same class that is used to store and read preferences on the Mac. You will have your applications use `NSUserDefaults` to read and store preference data using pairs of keys and values, just as you would access keyed data from an `NSDictionary`. The difference is that `NSUserDefaults` data is persisted to the file system rather than stored in an object instance in memory.

In this chapter, weÔÇÖre going to create an application, add and configure a settings bundle, and then access and edit those preferences from the Settings application and from within our own application.

One nice thing about the Settings application is that it provides a solution, so you donÔÇÖt need to design your own user interface for your preferences. You create a property list describing your applicationÔÇÖs available settings, and the Settings application creates the interface for you.

Immersive applications, such as games, generally should provide their own preferences view so that the user doesnÔÇÖt need to quit to make a change. Even utility and productivity applications might, at times, have preferences that a user should be able to change without leaving the application. WeÔÇÖll also show you to how to collect preferences from the user directly in your application and store those in iOSÔÇÖs User Defaults.

One additional complication is that the user can actually switch to the Settings application, change a preference, and then switch back to your still-running application. WeÔÇÖll show you how to handle that situation at the end of this chapter.

The Bridge Control Application

In this chapter, weÔÇÖre going to build a simple application that keeps track of some aspects of managing the bridge of a starship, which IÔÇÖm sure youÔÇÖll agree is a useful enterprise. Our first step will be to create a settings bundle so that, when the user launches the Settings application, there will be an entry for our application, Bridge Control (see [Figure 12-2](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig2)).

![9781484202005_Fig12-02.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-02.jpg)

[Figure 12-2](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig2). The Settings application, which shows an entry for our Bridge Control application in the simulator

If the user selects our application, Settings will drill down into a view that shows the preferences relevant to our application. As you can see in [Figure 12-3](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig3), the Settings application uses text fields, secure text fields, switches, and sliders to coax values out of our intrepid user.

![9781484202005_Fig12-03.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-03.jpg)

[Figure 12-3](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig3). Our applicationÔÇÖs primary settings view

Also notice the two items in the view that have disclosure indicators. The first one, *Rank*, takes the user to another table view that displays the options available for that item. From that table view, the user can select a single value (see [Figure 12-4](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig4)).

![9781484202005_Fig12-04.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-04.jpg)

[Figure 12-4](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig4). Selecting a single preference item from a list

The More Settings disclosure indicator allows the user to drill down to another set of preferences (see [Figure 12-5](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig5)). This child view can have the same kinds of controls as the main settings view and can even have its own child views. You may have noticed that the Settings application uses a navigation controller, which it needs because it supports the construction of hierarchical preference views.

![9781484202005_Fig12-05.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-05.jpg)

[Figure 12-5](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig5). A child settings view for our application

When users launch our application, they will be presented with a list of the preferences gathered in the Settings application (see [Figure 12-6](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig6)).

![9781484202005_Fig12-06.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-06.jpg)

[Figure 12-6](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig6). Our applicationÔÇÖs main view

To show how to update preferences from within our application, we also provide a second view where they can change additional preferences directly in the application (see [Figure 12-7](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig7)).

![9781484202005_Fig12-07.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-07.jpg)

[Figure 12-7](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig7). Setting some preferences directly in our application

LetÔÇÖs get started building Bridge Control, shall we?

Creating the Project

In Xcode, press ![image](images/sym3.jpg)![image](images/flower.jpg)**N** or select **File** ![image](images/arrow.jpg) **New** ![image](images/arrow.jpg) **ProjectÔÇª**. When the new project assistant comes up, select **Application** from under the iOS heading in the left pane, click the **Tabbed Application** icon, and then click **Next**. On the next screen, name your project *Bridge Control*. Set Devices to *Universal*, and then click the **Next** button. Finally, choose a location for your project and click **Create**.

The Bridge Control application is based on the `UITabBarController` class that we used in [Chapter 7](#9781484202005_Ch07.xhtml). The template creates two tabs, which is all weÔÇÖll need. Each tab requires an icon. YouÔÇÖll find these in the *12 ÔÇô Images* folder in the example source code archive. In Xcode, select *Images.xcassets*, and then drag the *singleicon.imageset* and *doubleicon.imageset* folders from *12 ÔÇô Images* into the editing area.

Next, weÔÇÖll assign the icons to their tab bar items. Select *Main.storyboard* and youÔÇÖll see the tab bar controller and the two child controllers for its tabs, one labeled *First View*, the other *Second View*. Select the first child controller, and then click its tab bar item, which currently shows a round circle and the title *First*. In the Bar Item section of the Attributes Inspector, change the Title to *Main* and the Image to *singleicon*, as shown in [Figure 12-8](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig8). Now select the tab bar item for the second child controller and change the title from *Second* to *Settings* and the image from *second* to *doubleicon*. Finally, select *Images.xcassets* again and delete the `first` and `second` image sets that the template createdÔÇöwe donÔÇÖt need them anymore. ThatÔÇÖs enough work on the application itself for nowÔÇöbefore doing anything more, letÔÇÖs create its settings bundle.

![9781484202005_Fig12-08.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-08.jpg)

[Figure 12-8](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig8). Setting the icon for the first tab bar item

Working with the Settings Bundle

The Settings application uses the contents of each applicationÔÇÖs settings bundle to construct a settings view for that application. If an application has no settings bundle, then the Settings app doesnÔÇÖt show anything for it. Each settings bundle must contain a property list called *Root.plist* that defines the root-level preferences view. This property list must follow a very precise format, which weÔÇÖll talk about when we set up the property list for our appÔÇÖs settings bundle.

When the Settings application starts up, it checks each application for a settings bundle and adds a settings group for each application that includes a settings bundle. If we want our preferences to include any subviews, we need to add property lists to the bundle and add an entry to *Root.plist* for each child view. YouÔÇÖll see exactly how to do that in this chapter.

Adding a Settings Bundle to Our Project

In the Project Navigator, click the *Bridge Control* folder, and then select **File** ![image](images/arrow.jpg) **New** ![image](images/arrow.jpg) **FileÔÇª** or press ![image](images/flower.jpg)**N**. In the left pane, select **Resource** under the iOS heading, and then select the **Settings Bundle** icon (see [Figure 12-9](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig9)). Click the **Next** button, leave the default name of *Settings.bundle*, and click **Create**.

![9781484202005_Fig12-09.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-09.jpg)

[Figure 12-9](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig9). Creating a settings bundle in Xcode

You should now see a new item in the project window called *Settings.bundle*. Expand the *Settings.bundle* item, and you should see two subitems: a folder named *en.lproj* that contains a file named *Root.strings*, and another named *Root.plist*. WeÔÇÖll discuss *en.lproj* in [Chapter 22](#9781484202005_Ch22.xhtml) when we talk about localizing your application into other languages. Here, weÔÇÖll concentrate on *Root.plist*.

Setting Up the Property List

Select *Root.plist* and take a look at the editor pane. YouÔÇÖre looking at XcodeÔÇÖs property list editor (see [Figure 12-10](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig10)).

![9781484202005_Fig12-10.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-10.jpg)

[Figure 12-10](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig10). Root.plist in the property list editor pane. If your editing pane looks slightly different, donÔÇÖt panic. Simply Control-click in the editing pane and select Show Raw Keys/Values from the contextual menu that appears

Notice the organization of the items in the property list. Property lists are essentially dictionaries, storing item types and values and using a key to retrieve them, just as an `NSDictionary` does.

Several different types of nodes can be put into a property list. The *Boolean*, *Data*, *Date*, *Number*, and *String* node types are meant to hold individual pieces of data, but you also have a couple of ways to deal with whole collections of nodes, as well. In addition to *Dictionary* node types, which allow you to store other dictionaries, there are *Array* nodes, which store an ordered list of other nodes similar to an `NSArray`. The `Dictionary` and `Array` types are the only property list node types that can contain other nodes.

**Note**┬á┬áAlthough you can use most kinds of objects as keys in an `NSDictionary`, keys in property list dictionary nodes must be strings. However, you are free to use any node type for the values.

When creating a settings property list, you need to follow a very specific format. Fortunately, *Root.plist*, the property list that came with the settings bundle you just added to your project, follows this format exactly. LetÔÇÖs take a look.

In the *Root.plist* editor pane, names of keys can either be displayed in their true, ÔÇ£rawÔÇØ form or in a slightly more human-readable form. WeÔÇÖre big fans of seeing things as they truly are whenever possible, so right-click anywhere in the editor and make sure the **Show Raw Keys/Values** option in the contextual menu is checked (see [Figure 12-11](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig11)). The rest of our discussion here uses the real names for all the keys weÔÇÖre going to talk about, so this step is important.

![9781484202005_Fig12-11.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-11.jpg)

[Figure 12-11](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig11). Control-click anywhere in the property list editing pane and make sure the Show Raw Keys/Values item is checked. This will ensure that real names are used in the property list editor, which makes your editing experience more precise

**Caution**┬á┬áAt the time of writing, leaving the property list, either by editing a different file or by quitting Xcode, resets the **Show Raw Keys/Values** item to be unchecked. If your text suddenly looks a little different, take another look at that menu item and make sure it is checked.

One of the items in the dictionary is *StringsTable*. A strings table is used in translating your application into another language. WeÔÇÖll discuss the translation of strings in [Chapter 22](#9781484202005_Ch22.xhtml) when we get into localization. We wonÔÇÖt be using it in this chapter, but feel free to leave it in your project since it wonÔÇÖt do any harm.

In addition to *StringsTable*, the property list contains a node named *PreferenceSpecifiers*, which is an array. This array node is designed to hold a set of dictionary nodes, where each node represents either a single preference item that the user can modify or a single child view that the user can drill down into.

Click the disclosure triangle to the left of *PreferenceSpecifiers* to expand that node. YouÔÇÖll notice that XcodeÔÇÖs template kindly gave us four child nodes (see [Figure 12-12](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig12)). Those nodes arenÔÇÖt likely to reflect our actual preferences, so delete *Item 1*, *Item 2*, and *Item 3* (select each one and press the **Delete** key, one after another), leaving just *Item 0* in place.

![9781484202005_Fig12-12.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-12.jpg)

[Figure 12-12](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig12). Root.plist in the editor pane, this time with PreferenceSpecifiers expanded

**Note**┬á┬áTo select an item in the property list, it is best to click one side or the other of the Key column to avoid bringing up the Key columnÔÇÖs drop-down menu.

Single-click **Item 0** but donÔÇÖt expand it. XcodeÔÇÖs property list editor lets you add rows simply by pressing the **Return** key. The current selection stateÔÇöincluding which row is selected and whether itÔÇÖs expandedÔÇödetermines where the new row will be inserted. When an unexpanded array or dictionary is selected, pressing **Return** adds a sibling node after the selected row. In other words, it will add another node at the same level as the current selection. If you were to press **Return** (but donÔÇÖt do that now), you would get a new row called *Item 1* immediately after Item 0\. [Figure 12-13](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig13) shows an example of hitting **Return** to create a new row. Notice the drop-down menu that allows you to specify the kind of preference specifier this item representsÔÇömore on this in a bit.

![9781484202005_Fig12-13.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-13.jpg)

[Figure 12-13](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig13). We selected Item 0 and hit Return to create a new sibling row. Note the drop-down menu that appears, allowing us to specify the kind of preference specifier this item represents

Now expand Item 0 and see what it contains (see [Figure 12-14](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig14)). The editor is now ready to add child nodes to the selected item. If you were to press **Return** at this point (again, donÔÇÖt actually press it now), you would get a new first child row inside Item 0.

![9781484202005_Fig12-14.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-14.jpg)

[Figure 12-14](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig14). When you expand Item 0, youÔÇÖll find a row with a key of Type and a second row with a key of Title. This represents a group with a title of Group

One of the items inside Item 0 has a key of *Type*. Every property list node in the *PreferenceSpecifiers*array must have an entry with this key. The *Type* key is typically the second entry, but order doesnÔÇÖt matter in a dictionary, so the *Type* key doesnÔÇÖt need to be second. The *Type* key tells the Settings application what type of data is associated with this item.

In Item 0, the *Type* item has a value of *PSGroupSpecifier*. This indicates that the item represents the start of a new group. Each item that follows will be part of this groupÔÇöuntil the next item with a Type of *PSGroupSpecifier*.

If you look back at [Figure 12-3](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig3), youÔÇÖll see that the Settings application presents the application settings in a grouped table. Item 0 in the *PreferenceSpecifiers* array in a settings bundle property list should always be a *PSGroupSpecifier*, so that the settings start in a new group. This is important because you need at least one group in every Settings table.

The only other entry in Item 0 has a key of *Title*, and this is used to set an optional header just above the group that is being started.

Now take a closer look at the Item 0 row itself, and youÔÇÖll see that itÔÇÖs actually shown as *Item 0 (Group ÔÇô Group)*. The values in parentheses represent the value of the *Type* item (the first *Group*) and the *Title* item (the second *Group*). This is a nice shortcut that Xcode gives you so that you can visually scan the contents of a settings bundle.

As shown back in [Figure 12-3](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig3), we called our first group *General Info*. Double-click the value next to Title, and change it from *Group* to *General Info* (see [Figure 12-15](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig15)). When you enter the new title, you may notice a slight change to Item 0\. ItÔÇÖs now shown as *Item 0 (Group ÔÇô General Info)* to reflect the new title. In the Settings application, the title is shown in uppercase, so the user will actually see GENERAL INFO instead. You can see this in [Figure 12-3](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig3).

![9781484202005_Fig12-15.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-15.jpg)

[Figure 12-15](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig15). We changed the title of the Item 0 group from Group to General Info

Adding a Text Field Setting

We now need to add a second item in this array, which will represent the first actual preference field. WeÔÇÖre going to start with a simple text field.

If you single-click the **PreferenceSpecifiers** row in the editor pane (donÔÇÖt do this, just keep reading) and press **Return** to add a child, the new row will be inserted at the beginning of the list, which is not what we want. We want to add a row at the end of the array.

To add the row, click the disclosure triangle to the left of Item 0 to close it, and then select **Item 0** and press **Return**. This gives you a new sibling row after the current row (see [Figure 12-16](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig16)). As usual, when the item is added, a drop-down menu appears, showing the default value of *Text Field*.

![9781484202005_Fig12-16.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-16.jpg)

[Figure 12-16](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig16). Adding a new sibling row to Item 0

Click somewhere outside the drop-down menu to make it go away, and then click the disclosure triangle next to Item 1 to expand it. YouÔÇÖll see that it contains a Type row set to *PSTextFieldSpecifier*. This is the *Type* value used to tell the Settings application that we want the user to edit this setting in a text field. It also contains two empty rows for Title and Key (see [Figure 12-17](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#Fig17)).

![9781484202005_Fig12-17.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig12-17.jpg)

[Figure 12-17](#9781484202005_Ch12.xhtml#_Fig17). Our text field item, expanded to show the type, title, and key

Select the **Title** row, and then double-click in the whitespace of the Value column. Type in **Commanding Officer** to set the Title value. This is the text that will appear in the Settings app.

Now do the same for the Key row (no, thatÔÇÖs not a misprint, youÔÇÖre really looking at a key called *Key*). For a value, type in **officer** (note the lowercase first letter). Remember that user defaults work like an `NSDictionary`. This entry tells the Settings application which key to use when it stores the value entered in this text field.

Recall what we said about `NSUserDefaults`? It lets you store values using a key, similar to an `NSDictionary`. Well, the Settings application will do the same thing for each of the preferences it saves on your behalf. If you give it a key value of *foo*, then, later in your application, you can request the value for *foo*, and it will give you the value the user entered for that preference. We will use this same key value later to retrieve this setting from the user defaults in our application.

**Note**┬á┬áOur Title has a value of *Commanding Officer* and our Key has a value of *officer*. This uppercase/lowercase difference will happen frequently, and here weÔÇÖre even compounding the difference by using two words for the displayed title, and a single word for the key. The *Title* is what appears on the screen; so the capital C and O, and putting a space between the words, all makes sense. The *Key* is a text string weÔÇÖll use to retrieve preferences from the user defaults, so all lowercase makes sense there. Could we use all lowercase for *Title*? You bet.
