![9781484202005_Fig14-01.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-01.jpg)

[Figure 14-1](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig1). Editing an extremely low-resolution icon in TinyPix

Start off by creating a new project in Xcode. From the **iOS Application** section, select the **Master-Detail Application** template and then click **Next**. Name this new app *TinyPix* and set the **Devices** pop-up to *Universal*. Make sure the **Use Core Data** check box is unchecked. Now click **Next** again and choose the location to save your project.

In XcodeÔÇÖs Project Navigator, youÔÇÖll see that your project contains files for `AppDelegate`, `MasterViewController`, and `DetailViewController`, as well as the *Main.storyboard* file. WeÔÇÖll make changes to all of these files and we will create a few new classes along the way, as well.

Creating TinyPixDocument

The first new class weÔÇÖre going to create is the document class that will contain the data for each TinyPix image thatÔÇÖs loaded from file storage. Select the *TinyPix* folder in Xcode and press ![image](images/flower.jpg)**N** to create a new file. From the **iOS** section, select **Cocoa Touch Class** and click **Next**. Enter **TinyPixDocument** in the **Class** field, enter **UIDocument** in the **Subclass of** field, and click **Next**. Finally, click **Create** to create the files.

LetÔÇÖs think about the public API of this class before we get into its implementation details. This class is going to represent an 8 ├ù 8 grid of pixels, where each pixel consists of a single on or off value. So, letÔÇÖs give it a method that takes a pair of row and column indexes and returns a `BOOL` value. LetÔÇÖs also provide a method to set a specific state at a specified row and column, and as a convenience, another method that simply toggles the state at a particular place.

Select *TinyPixDocument.h* to edit the new classÔÇÖs header. Add the following bold lines:

```
#import <UIKit/UIKit.h>

@interface TinyPixDocument :┬áUIDocument

// row and column range from 0┬áto 7
- (BOOL)stateAtRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column;
- (void)setState:(BOOL)state atRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column;
- (void)toggleStateAtRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column;

@end
```

Now switch over to *TinyPixDocument.m*, where weÔÇÖll implement storage for our 8 ├ù 8 grid, the methods defined in our public API, and the required `UIDocument` methods that will enable loading and saving our documents.

LetÔÇÖs start by defining the storage for our 8 ├ù 8 bitmap data. WeÔÇÖll hold this data in an instance of `NSMutableData`, which lets us work directly with an array of byte data that is still contained inside an object, so that the usual Cocoa memory management will take care of freeing the memory when weÔÇÖre finished with it. Add this class extension to make it happen:

```
#import "TinyPixDocument.h"

@interface TinyPixDocument ()

@property (strong, nonatomic) NSMutableData *bitmap;

@end

@implementation TinyPixDocument
```

The `UIDocument` class has a designated initializer that all subclasses should use. This is where weÔÇÖll create our initial bitmap. In true bitmap style, weÔÇÖre going to minimize memory usage by using a single byte to contain each row. Each bit in the byte represents the on/off value of a column index within that row. In total, our document contains just 8 bytes.

**Note**┬á┬áThis section contains a small number of bitwise operations, as well as some C pointer and array manipulation. This is all pretty mundane for C developers; but if you donÔÇÖt have much C experience, it may seem puzzling or even impenetrable. In that case, feel free to simply copy and use the code provided (it works just fine). If you really want to understand whatÔÇÖs going on, you may want to dig deeper into C itself, perhaps by adding a copy of *Learn C on the Mac* by Dave Mark (Apress, 2009) to your bookshelf.

Add this method to our documentÔÇÖs implementation, placing it directly above the `@end` at the bottom of the file:

```
- (id)initWithFileURL:(NSURL *)url {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself =┬á[super initWithFileURL:url];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áunsigned char startPattern[] =┬á{
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x01,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x02,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x04,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x08,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x10,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x20,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x40,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á0x80
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á};

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.bitmap =┬á[NSMutableData dataWithBytes:startPattern length:8];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

This starts off each bitmap with a simple diagonal pattern stretching from one corner to another.

Now, itÔÇÖs time to implement the methods that make up the public API we defined in the header. LetÔÇÖs tackle the method for reading the state of a single bit first. This simply grabs the relevant byte from our array of bytes, and then does a bit shift and an `AND` operation to determine whether the specified bit was set, returning `YES` or `NO` accordingly. Add this method above the `@end`:

```
- (BOOL)stateAtRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column {
┬á┬á┬á┬áconst char *bitmapBytes =┬á[self.bitmap bytes];
┬á┬á┬á┬áchar rowByte =┬ábitmapBytes[row];
┬á┬á┬á┬áchar result =┬á(1 << column) & rowByte;
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (result != 0) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn NO;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Next comes the **inverse**: a method that sets the value specified at a given row and column. Here, we once again grab the relevant byte for the specified row and do a bit shift. But this time, instead of using the shifted bit to examine the contents of the row, we use it to either set or unset a bit in the row. Add this method above the `@end`:

```
- (void)setState:(BOOL)state atRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column {
┬á┬á┬á┬áchar *bitmapBytes =┬á[self.bitmap mutableBytes];
┬á┬á┬á┬áchar *rowByte = &bitmapBytes[row];

┬á┬á┬á┬áif (state) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á*rowByte =┬á*rowByte |┬á(1 << column);
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á*rowByte =┬á*rowByte & ~(1 << column);
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Now, letÔÇÖs add a convenience method that lets outside code simply toggle a single cell:

```
- (void)toggleStateAtRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column {
┬á┬á┬á┬áBOOL state =┬á[self stateAtRow:row column:column];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setState:!state atRow:row column:column];
}
```

Our document class requires two final pieces before it fits into the puzzle of a document-based app: methods for reading and writing. As we mentioned earlier, you donÔÇÖt need to deal with files directly. You donÔÇÖt even need to worry about the URL that was passed into the `initWithFileURL:` method earlier. All that you need to do is implement one method that transforms the documentÔÇÖs data structure into an `NSData` object, ready for saving, and another that takes a freshly loaded `NSData` object and pulls the objectÔÇÖs data structure out of it. Because our documentÔÇÖs internal structure is already contained in an `NSMutableData` object, which is a subclass of `NSData`, these implementations are pleasingly simple. Add these two methods above the `@end`:

```
- (id)contentsForType:(NSString *)typeName error:(NSError **)outError {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"saving document to URL %@", self.fileURL);
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [self.bitmap copy];
}

- (BOOL)loadFromContents:(id)contents ofType:(NSString *)typeName
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áerror:(NSError **)outError {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"loading document from URL %@", self.fileURL);
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.bitmap =┬á[contents mutableCopy];
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn true;
}
```

The first of these methods, `contentsForType:error:`, is called whenever our document is about to be saved to storage. It simply returns an immutable copy of our bitmap data, which the system will take care of storing later.

The second method, `loadFromContents:ofType:error:`, is called whenever the system has just loaded data from storage and wants to provide this data to an instance of our document class. Here, we just grab a mutable copy of the data that has been passed in. WeÔÇÖve included some logging statements, just so you can see whatÔÇÖs happening in the Xcode log later on.

Each of these methods allows you to do some things that weÔÇÖre ignoring in this app. They both provide a `typeName` parameter, which you could use to distinguish between different types of data storage that your document can load from or save to. They also have an `outError` parameter, which you could use to specify that an error occurred while copying data to or from your documentÔÇÖs in-memory data structure. In our case, however, what weÔÇÖre doing is so simple that these arenÔÇÖt important concerns.

ThatÔÇÖs all we need for our document class. Sticking to MVC principles, our document sits squarely in the model camp, knowing nothing about how itÔÇÖs displayed. And thanks to the `UIDocument` superclass, the document is even shielded from most of the details about how itÔÇÖs stored.

Code Master

Now that we have our document class ready to go, itÔÇÖs time to address the first view that a user sees when running our app: the list of existing TinyPix documents, which is taken care of by the `MasterViewController` class. We need to let this class know how to grab the list of available documents, let the user choose an existing document for viewing or editing, and create and name a new document. When a document is created or chosen, itÔÇÖs then passed along to the detail controller for display.

Start by selecting *MasterViewController.m*. This file, generated as part of the MasterÔÇôDetail application template, contains starter code for displaying an array of items. WeÔÇÖre not going to use any of that, but instead do these things all on our own. Therefore, delete all the methods from the `@implementation` block and all the declarations in the class extension at the top. When youÔÇÖre done, you should have a clean slate that looks something like this:

```
#import "MasterViewController.h"
#import "DetailViewController.h"

@interface MasterViewController ()
@end

@implementation MasterViewController
@end
```

WeÔÇÖll also include a segmented control in our GUI, which will allow the user to choose a tint color that will be used as a highlight color for portions of the TinyPix GUI. Although this is not a particularly useful feature in and of itself, it will help demonstrate the iCloud mechanism, as the highlight color setting makes its way from the device on which you set it to another of your connected devices running the same app. The first version of the app will use the color as a local setting on each device. Later in the chapter, weÔÇÖll add the code to make the color setting propagate through iCloud to the userÔÇÖs other devices.

To implement the color selection control, weÔÇÖll add an outlet and an action to our code as well. WeÔÇÖll also add properties for holding onto a list of document file names and a pointer to the document the user has chosen. Make these changes to *MasterViewController.m*:

```
#import "MasterViewController.h"
#import "DetailViewController.h"
#import "TinyPixDocument.h"

@interface MasterViewController ()

@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet UISegmentedControl *colorControl;
@property (strong, nonatomic) NSArray *documentFilenames;
@property (strong, nonatomic) TinyPixDocument *chosenDocument;

@end
```

Before we implement the table view methods and other standard methods that we need to deal with, we are going to write a couple of private utility methods. The first of these takes a file name, combines it with the file path of the appÔÇÖs *Documents* directory, and returns a URL pointing to that specific file. As you saw in [Chapter 13](#9781484202005_Ch13.xhtml), the *Documents* directory is a special location that iOS sets aside, one for each app installed on an iOS device. You can use it to store documents created by your app, and rest assured that those documents will be automatically included whenever users back up their iOS device, whether itÔÇÖs to iTunes or iCloud.

Add this method to the implementation, placing it directly above the `@end` at the bottom of the file:

```
- (NSURL *)urlForFilename:(NSString *)filename {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSFileManager *fm =┬á[NSFileManager defaultManager];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSArray *urls =┬á[fm URLsForDirectory:NSDocumentDirectory
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á inDomains:NSUserDomainMask];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *directoryURL =┬áurls[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *fileURL =┬á[directoryURL URLByAppendingPathComponent:filename];
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn fileURL;
}
```

Here we are using a method of the `NSFileManager` class to get a URL that maps to the applicationÔÇÖs *Documents* directory. This method works just like the `NSSearchPathForDirectoriesInDomains()` function that we used in [Chapter 13](#9781484202005_Ch13.xhtml), except that it returns an array of `NSURL` objects instead of strings, which is more convenient for the purposes of this method.

The second private method is a bit longer. It also uses the *Documents* directory, this time to search for files representing existing documents. The method takes the files it finds and sorts them by creation date, so that the user will see the list of documents sorted ÔÇ£blog-styleÔÇØ with the newest items first. The document file names are stashed away in the `documentFilenames` property, and then the table view (which we admittedly havenÔÇÖt yet dealt with) is reloaded. Add this method above the `@end`:

```
- (void)reloadFiles {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSArray *paths =┬áNSSearchPathForDirectoriesInDomains(NSDocumentDirectory,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDomainMask, YES);
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *path =┬ápaths[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSFileManager *fm =┬á[NSFileManager defaultManager];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSError *dirError;
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSArray *files =┬á[fm contentsOfDirectoryAtPath:path error:&dirError];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!files) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"Error listing files in directory %@: %@",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ápath, dirError);
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"found files: %@", files);

┬á┬á┬á┬áfiles =┬á[files sortedArrayUsingComparator:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ^NSComparisonResult(id filename1, id filename2) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *attr1 =┬á[fm attributesOfItemAtPath:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [path stringByAppendingPathComponent:filename1]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á error:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *attr2 =┬á[fm attributesOfItemAtPath:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [path stringByAppendingPathComponent:filename2]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á error:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [attr2[NSFileCreationDate] compare: attr1[NSFileCreationDate]];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.documentFilenames =┬áfiles;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.tableView reloadData];
}
```

Now, letÔÇÖs deal with our dear old friends, the table view data source methods. These should be pretty familiar to you by now. Add the following three methods above the `@end`:

```
- (NSInteger)numberOfSectionsInTableView:(UITableView *)tableView {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn 1;
}

- (NSInteger)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ánumberOfRowsInSection:(NSInteger)section {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [self.documentFilenames count];
}

- (UITableViewCell *)tableView:(UITableView *)tableView
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácellForRowAtIndexPath:(NSIndexPath *)indexPath {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUITableViewCell *cell =┬á[tableView dequeueReusableCellWithIdentifier:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á @"FileCell"];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *path =┬áself.documentFilenames[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬ácell.textLabel.text =┬ápath.lastPathComponent.stringByDeletingPathExtension;
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn cell;
}
```

These methods are based on the contents of the array stored in the `documentFilenames` property. The `tableView:cellForForAtIndexPath:` method relies on the existence of a cell attached to the table view with `"FileCell"` set as its identifier, so we must be sure to set that up in the storyboard a little later.

If not for the fact that we havenÔÇÖt touched our storyboard yet, the code we have now would almost be something we could run and see in action; however, with no preexisting TinyPix documents, we would have nothing to display in our table view. And so far, we donÔÇÖt have any way to create new documents, either. Also, we have not yet dealt with the color-selection control weÔÇÖre going to add. So, letÔÇÖs do a bit more work before we try to run our app.

The userÔÇÖs choice of highlight color will be used to immediately set a tint color for the segmented control. The `UIView` class has a `tintColor` property. When itÔÇÖs set for any view, the value applies to that view and will propagate down to all of its subviews. When we set the segmented controlÔÇÖs tint color, weÔÇÖll also store it in `NSUserDefaults` for later retrieval. Add these two methods above the `@end`:

```
- (IBAction)chooseColor:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSInteger selectedColorIndex =┬á[(UISegmentedControl *)sender
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselectedSegmentIndex];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setTintColorForIndex:selectedColorIndex];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDefaults *prefs =┬á[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[prefs setInteger:selectedColorIndex forKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[prefs synchronize];
}

- (void)setTintColorForIndex:(NSInteger)selectedColorIndex {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.colorControl.tintColor =┬á[TinyPixUtils getTintColorForIndex:selectedColorIndex];
}
```

The first method is triggered when the user changes the selection in the segmented control. It saves the selected index in the user defaults and passes it to the second method, which converts the index to a color and applies it to the segmented control. WeÔÇÖll need the code that does the conversion from index to color in the detail view controller as well, so itÔÇÖs implemented in a separate class. To create that class, press ![image](images/flower.jpg)**N** to open the new file dialog. From the **iOS** section, select **Cocoa Touch Class** and click **Next**. Enter **TinyPixUtils** in the **Class** field, enter **NSObject** in the **Subclass of** field, and click **Next**. Finally, click **Create** to create the files.

The `TinyPixUtils` class will have a single method. Edit *TinyPixUtils.h* to add the declaration of that method:

```
#import <UIKit/UIKit.h>

@interface TinyPixUtils :┬áNSObject

+ (UIColor *)getTintColorForIndex:(NSUInteger)index;

@end
```

Now switch over to *TinyPixUtils.m* to add the method implementation:

```
#import "TinyPixUtils.h"

@implementation TinyPixUtils

+ (UIColor *)getTintColorForIndex:(NSUInteger)index {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIColor *color =┬á[UIColor redColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬áswitch (index) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácase 0:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácolor =┬á[UIColor redColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábreak;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácase 1:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácolor =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[UIColor colorWithRed:0 green:0.6 blue:0 alpha:1];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábreak;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácase 2:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácolor =┬á[UIColor blueColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábreak;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádefault:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábreak;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn color;
}

@end
```

We realize that we havenÔÇÖt yet set anything up in the storyboard, but weÔÇÖll get there! First, we have some more work to do in *MasterViewController.m*. Start by adding an import for *TinyPixUtils.h*:

```
#import "MasterViewController.h"
#import "DetailViewController.h"
#import "TinyPixDocument.h"
#import "TinyPixUtils.h"

@interface MasterViewController ()
```

Now letÔÇÖs work on the `viewDidLoad` method. After calling the superclassÔÇÖs implementation, weÔÇÖll start by adding a button to the right side of the navigation bar. The user will press this button to create a new TinyPix document. WeÔÇÖll also load the saved tint color from the user defaults and use it to set the tint color of the segmented control. We finish by calling the `reloadFiles` method that we implemented earlier.

Add this code to implement `viewDidLoad`:

```
- (void)viewDidLoad {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewDidLoad];

┬á┬á┬á┬áUIBarButtonItem *addButton =┬á[[UIBarButtonItem alloc]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áinitWithBarButtonSystemItem:UIBarButtonSystemItemAdd
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átarget:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áaction:@selector(insertNewObject)];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.navigationItem.rightBarButtonItem =┬áaddButton;

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDefaults *prefs =┬á[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSInteger selectedColorIndex =┬á[prefs integerForKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setTintColorForIndex:selectedColorIndex];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.colorControl setSelectedSegmentIndex:selectedColorIndex];

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self reloadFiles];
}
```

As youÔÇÖll see when you run the app for the first time, the segmented controlÔÇÖs tint color starts out being red. ThatÔÇÖs because thereÔÇÖs nothing stored in the user defaults yet, so the `integerForKey:` method returns 0, which the `setTintColorForIndex:` method interprets as red.

You may have noticed that, when we created the `UIBarButtonItem`, we told it to call the `insertNewObject` method when itÔÇÖs pressed. We havenÔÇÖt written that method yet, so letÔÇÖs do so now. Add this method above the `@end`:

```
- (void)insertNewObject {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIAlertController *alert =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [UIAlertController alertControllerWithTitle:@"Choose File Name"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á message: @"Enter a┬áname for your new TinyPix document."
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á preferredStyle: UIAlertControllerStyleAlert];

┬á┬á┬á┬á[alert addTextFieldWithConfigurationHandler:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIAlertAction *cancelAction =┬á[UIAlertAction actionWithTitle:@"Cancel"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á style:UIAlertActionStyleCancel handler:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIAlertAction *createAction =┬á[UIAlertAction actionWithTitle:@"Create"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á style:UIAlertActionStyleDefault handler:^(UIAlertAction *action) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á UITextField *textField =┬á(UITextField *)alert.textFields[0];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [self createFileNamed:textField.text];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á }];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[alert addAction:cancelAction];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[alert addAction:createAction];

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self presentViewController:alert animated:YES completion:nil];
}
```

This method uses the `UIAlertController` class to display an alert that includes a text-input field, a **Create** button, and a **Cancel** button. If the **Create** button is pressed, the responsibility of creating a new item instead falls to the method that the buttonÔÇÖs handler block calls when itÔÇÖs finished, which weÔÇÖll also address now. Add this method above the `@end`:

```
- (void)createFileNamed:(NSString *)fileName {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *trimmedFileName =┬á[fileName
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ástringByTrimmingCharactersInSet:[NSCharacterSet whitespaceCharacterSet]];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (trimmedFileName.length > 0) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *targetName =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"%@.tinypix",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á trimmedFileName];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *saveUrl =┬á[self urlForFilename:targetName];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.chosenDocument =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [[TinyPixDocument alloc] initWithFileURL:saveUrl];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.chosenDocument saveToURL:saveUrl
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áforSaveOperation:UIDocumentSaveForCreating
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á completionHandler:^(BOOL success) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á if (success) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á NSLog(@"save OK");
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [self reloadFiles];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [self performSegueWithIdentifier:@"masterToDetail"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á sender:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á } else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á NSLog(@"failed to save!");
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á }
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á }];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

This method starts out simply enough. It strips leading and trailing whitespace characters from the name that itÔÇÖs passed. If the result is not empty, it then creates a file name based on the userÔÇÖs entry, a URL based on that file name (using the `urlForFilename:` method we wrote earlier), and a new `TinyPixDocument` instance using that URL.

What comes next is a little more subtle. ItÔÇÖs important to understand here that just creating a new document with a given URL doesnÔÇÖt create the file. In fact, at the time that the `initWithFileURL:` is called, the document doesnÔÇÖt yet know if the given URL refers to an existing file or to a new file that needs to be created. We need to tell it what to do. In this case, we tell it to save a new file at the given URL with this code:

```
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.chosenDocument saveToURL:saveUrl
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áforSaveOperation:UIDocumentSaveForCreating
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á completionHandler:^(BOOL success) {
.
.
.
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}];
```

Of interest is the purpose and usage of the block that is passed in as the last argument. The method weÔÇÖre calling, `saveToURL:forSaveOperation:completionHandler:`, doesnÔÇÖt have a return value to tell us how it all worked out. In fact, the method returns immediately after itÔÇÖs called, long before the file is actually saved. Instead, it starts the file-saving work, and later, when itÔÇÖs done, calls the block that we gave it, using the `success` parameter to let us know whether it succeeded. To make it all work as smoothly as possible, the file-saving work is actually performed on a background thread. The block we pass in, however, is executed on the thread that called `saveToURL:forSaveOperation:completionHandler:` in the first place. In this particular case, that means that the block is executed on the main thread, so we can safely use any facilities that require the main thread, such as UIKit. With that in mind, take a look again at what happens inside that block:

```
if (success) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"save OK");
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self reloadFiles];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self performSegueWithIdentifier:@"masterToDetail"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ásender:self];
} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"failed to save!");
}
```

This is the content of the block we passed in to the file-saving method, and itÔÇÖs called later, after the file operation is completed. We check to see if it succeeded; if so, we do an immediate file reload, and then initiate a segue to another view controller. This is an aspect of segues that we didnÔÇÖt cover in [Chapter 9](#9781484202005_Ch09.xhtml), but itÔÇÖs pretty straightforward.

The idea is that a segue in a storyboard file can have an identifier, just like a table view cell, and you can use that identifier to trigger a segue programmatically. In this case, weÔÇÖll just need to remember to configure that segue in the storyboard when we get to it. But before we do that, letÔÇÖs add the last method this class needs, to take care of that segue. Insert this method above the `@end`:

```
- (void)prepareForSegue:(UIStoryboardSegue *)segue sender:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *destination =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(UINavigationController *)segue.destinationViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áDetailViewController *detailVC =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(DetailViewController *)destination.topViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (sender == self) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// if sender == self, a┬ánew document has just been created,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// and chosenDocument is already set.
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádetailVC.detailItem =┬áself.chosenDocument;
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// find the chosen document from the tableview
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSIndexPath *indexPath =┬á[self.tableView indexPathForSelectedRow];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *filename =┬áself.documentFilenames[indexPath.row];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *docUrl =┬á[self urlForFilename:filename];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.chosenDocument =┬á[[TinyPixDocument alloc]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á initWithFileURL:docUrl];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.chosenDocument openWithCompletionHandler:^(BOOL success) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (success) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"load OK");
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádetailVC.detailItem =┬áself.chosenDocument;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"failed to load!");
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

This method has two clear paths of execution that are determined by the condition at the top. Remember from our discussion of storyboards in [Chapter 9](#9781484202005_Ch09.xhtml) that this method is called on a view controller whenever a segue is about to performed from that view controller. The `sender` parameter refers to the object that initiated the segue, and we use that to figure out just what to do here. If the segue is initiated by the programmatic method call we performed in the alert view delegate method, then `sender` will be equal to `self`, because thatÔÇÖs the value of the `sender` argument in the `performSegueWithIdentifier:sender:` call in the `createFileNamed:` method. In that case, we know that the `chosenDocument` property is already set, and we simply pass its value to the destination view controller.

Otherwise, we know weÔÇÖre responding to the user touching a row in the table view, and thatÔÇÖs where things get a little more complicated. ThatÔÇÖs the time to construct a URL (much as we did when creating a document), create a new instance of our document class, and try to open the file. YouÔÇÖll see that the method we call to open the file, `openWithCompletionHandler:`, works similarly to the save method we used earlier. We pass it a block that it will save for later execution. Just as with the file-saving method, the loading occurs in the background, and this block will be executed on the main thread when itÔÇÖs complete. At that point, if the loading succeeded, we pass the document along to the detail view controller.

Note that both of these methods use the key-value coding technique that weÔÇÖve used a few times before, letting us set the `detailItem` property of the segueÔÇÖs destination controller, even though we donÔÇÖt include its header. This will work out just fine for us, since `DetailViewController`ÔÇöthe detail view controller class created as part of the Xcode projectÔÇöhappens to include a property called `detailItem` right out of the box.

With the amount of code we now have in place, itÔÇÖs high time we configured the storyboard so that we can run our app and make something happen. Save your code and continue.

Initial Storyboarding

Select *Main.storyboard* in the Xcode Project Navigator and take a look at whatÔÇÖs already there. YouÔÇÖll find scenes for a split view controller, two navigation controllers, the master view controller, and the detail view controller (see [Figure 14-2](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig2)). All of our work will be with the master and detail view controllers.

![9781484202005_Fig14-02.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-02.jpg)

[Figure 14-2](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig2). The TinyPix storyboard, showing split view controller, navigation controllers, master view controller, and detail view controller

LetÔÇÖs start by dealing with the master view controller scene. This is where the table view showing the list of all our TinyPix documents is configured. By default, this sceneÔÇÖs table view is configured to use dynamic cells instead of static cells. We want our table view to get its contents from the data source methods we implemented, so this default setting is just what we want. We do need to configure the cell prototype though, so select it, and open the Attributes Inspector. Change the cellÔÇÖs **Identifier** from *Cell* to *FileCell*. This will let the data source code we wrote earlier access the table view cell.

We also need to create the segue that weÔÇÖre triggering in our code. Do this by Control-dragging from the master view controllerÔÇÖs icon (a yellow circle at the top of its scene or the **Master** icon under Master Scene in the Document Outline) over to the Navigation Controller for the detail view, and then selecting **Show Detail** from the storyboard segues menu.

YouÔÇÖll now see two segues that seem to connect the two scenes. By selecting each of them, you can tell where theyÔÇÖre coming from. Selecting one segue highlights the whole master scene; selecting the second one highlights just the table view cell. Select the segue that highlights the whole scene (i.e., the segue that you just created), and use the Attributes Inspector to set its **Identifier**, which is currently empty, to *masterToDetail*.

The final touch needed for the master view controller scene is to let the user pick which color will be used to represent an ÔÇ£onÔÇØ point in the detail view. Instead of implementing some kind of comprehensive color picker, weÔÇÖre just going to add a segmented control that will let the user pick from a set of predefined colors.

Find a **Segmented Control** in the object library, drag it out, and place it in the navigation bar at the top of the master view (see [Figure 14-3](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig3)).

![9781484202005_Fig14-03.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-03.jpg)

[Figure 14-3](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig3). The TinyPix storyboard, showing the master view controller with a segmented control being dropped on the controllerÔÇÖs navigation bar

Make sure the segmented control is selected and then open the Attributes Inspector. In the **Segmented Control** section at the top of the inspector, use the stepper control to change the number of Segments from *2* to *3*. Next, double-click the title of each segment in turn, changing them to *Red*, *Green*, and *Blue*, respectively. After setting those titles, click one of the resizing handles for the segmented control to make it fill out to the right width.

Next, Control-drag from the segmented control to the icon representing the master controller (the yellow circle labeled *Master* above the controller in the storyboard, or the Document Outline icon labeled *Master* under Master Scene) and select the **chooseColor:** method. Then Control-drag from the master controller back to the segmented control, and select the **colorControl** outlet.

WeÔÇÖve finally reached a point where we can run the app and see all our hard work brought to life! Run your app. YouÔÇÖll see it start up and display an empty table view with a segmented control at the top and a plus (+) button in the upper-right corner (see [Figure 14-4](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig4)).

![9781484202005_Fig14-04.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-04.jpg)

[Figure 14-4](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig4). The TinyPix app when it first appears. Click the plus icon to add a new document. YouÔÇÖll be prompted to name your new TinyPix document. At the moment, all the detail view does is display the document name in a label

Hit the **+** button, and the app will ask you to name the new document. Give it a name, tap **Create**, and youÔÇÖll see the app transition to the detail display, which is, well, under construction right now. All the default implementation of the detail view controller does is display the description of its `detailItem` in a label. Of course, thereÔÇÖs more information in the console view in Xcode. ItÔÇÖs not much, but itÔÇÖs something!

Tap the **Back** button to return to the master list, where youÔÇÖll see the item you added. Go ahead and create one or two more items to see that theyÔÇÖre correctly added to the list. Finally, head back to Xcode because weÔÇÖve got more work to do!

Creating TinyPixView

Our next order of business is the creation of a view class to display our grid and let the user edit it. Select the *TinyPix* folder in the Project Navigator, and press ![image](images/flower.jpg)**N** to create a new file. In the **iOS Source** section, select **Cocoa Touch Class** and click **Next**. Name the new class *TinyPixView* and choose **UIView** in the **Subclass of** pop-up. Click **Next**, verify that the save location is OK, and click **Create**.

**Note**┬á┬áThe implementation of our view class includes some drawing and touch handling that we havenÔÇÖt covered yet. Rather than bog down this chapter with too many details about these topics, weÔÇÖre just going to quickly show you the code. WeÔÇÖll cover details about drawing with Core Graphics in [Chapter 16](#9781484202005_Ch16.xhtml), and responding to touches and drags in [Chapter 18](#9781484202005_Ch18.xhtml).

Select *TinyPixView.h* and make the following changes:

```
#import <UIKit/UIKit.h>

@class TinyPixDocument;

@interface TinyPixView :┬áUIView

@property (strong, nonatomic) TinyPixDocument *document;

@end
```

All weÔÇÖre doing here is adding a property, so that the controller can pass along the document.

Now switch over to *TinyPixView.m*, where we have some more substantial work ahead of us. Start by adding this class extension at the top of the file:

```
#import "TinyPixView.h"
#import "TinyPixDocument.h"

typedef struct {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUInteger row;
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUInteger column;
} GridIndex;

@interface TinyPixView ()

@property (assign, nonatomic) CGSize lastSize;
@property (assign, nonatomic) CGRect gridRect;
@property (assign, nonatomic) CGSize blockSize;
@property (assign, nonatomic) CGFloat gap;
@property (assign, nonatomic) GridIndex selectedBlockIndex;

@end

@implementation TinyPixView
.
.
.
```

Here, we defined a C `struct` called `GridIndex` as a handy way to deal with row/column pairs. We also defined a class extension with some properties that weÔÇÖll need to use later.

A `UIView` subclass is usually initialized by calling its `initWithFrame:` method, which is its default initializer. However, since this class is going to be loaded from a storyboard, it will instead be initialized using the `initWithCoder:` method. WeÔÇÖll implement both of these methods, making each call a third method that initializes our properties. Add the following code to *TinyPixView.m*:

```
- (id)initWithFrame:(CGRect)frame {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself =┬á[super initWithFrame:frame];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Initialization code
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self commonInit];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}

- (id)initWithCoder:(NSCoder *)aDecoder {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself =┬á[super initWithCoder:aDecoder];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self commonInit];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}

- (void)commonInit{
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self calculateGridForSize:self.bounds.size];
┬á┬á┬á┬á_selectedBlockIndex.row =┬áNSNotFound;
┬á┬á┬á┬á_selectedBlockIndex.column =┬áNSNotFound;
}
```

The `calculateGridForSize:` method figures out how large the cells in the color grid should be, based on the size of `TinyPixView`. Calculating the grid size allows us to use the same application with screens of different sizes, and also handles the case where the size of the view changes when the device is rotated. Add the implementation of the `calculateGridForSize:` method to *TinyPixView.m*:

```
- (void)calculateGridForSize:(CGSize)size {
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat space =┬áMIN(size.width, size.height);
┬á┬á┬á┬á_gap =┬áspace/57;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat cellSide =┬á6 *┬á_gap;
┬á┬á┬á┬á_blockSize =┬áCGSizeMake(cellSide, cellSide);
┬á┬á┬á┬á_gridRect =┬áCGRectMake((size.width -┬áspace)/2,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (size.height -┬áspace)/2, space, space);
}
```

The idea behind this method is to make the grid fill either the full width or the full height of the view, whichever is the smaller, and to center it along the longer axis. To do that, we calculate the size of each cell, plus the gaps between the cells, by dividing the smaller dimension of the view by 57\. Why 57? Well, we want to have space for eight cells and we want each cell to be six times the size of the intercell gap. Given that we need gaps between each pair of cell, plus a gap at the start and end of each row or column, that effectively means we need space for (6 ├ù 8) + 9 = 57 gaps. Once we have the gap size, we get the size of each cell (by multiplying by 6). We use that information to set the value of the `blockSize` property, which represents the size of each cell, and the `gridRect` property, which corresponds to the region within the view in which the grid cells will actually be drawn.

Now letÔÇÖs take a look at the drawing routines. We override the standard `UIView drawRect:` method, use that to simply walk through all the blocks in our grid, and then call another method that will draw each cell block. Add the following bold code and donÔÇÖt forget to remove the comment marks around the `drawRect:` method:

```
/*
// Only override drawRect: if you perform custom drawing.
// An empty implementation adversely affects performance during animation.
- (void)drawRect:(CGRect)rect
{
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Drawing code
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!_document) return;

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGSize size =┬áself.bounds.size;
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!CGSizeEqualToSize(size, self.lastSize)) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.lastSize =┬ásize;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self calculateGridForSize:size];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}

┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (NSUInteger row =┬á0; row < 8; row++) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (NSUInteger column =┬á0; column < 8; column++) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self drawBlockAtRow:row column:column];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
*/
```

Before we draw the cells, we compare the current size of the view to the value in the `lastSize` property, and if itÔÇÖs different, we call `calculateGridForSize:`. This will happen when the view is first drawn and any time it changes size, which will most likely be when the device is rotated.

Now add the code that draws the block for each cell in the grid:

```
- (void)drawBlockAtRow:(NSUInteger)row column:(NSUInteger)column {
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat startX =┬á_gridRect.origin.x +┬á_gap
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á+ (_blockSize.width +┬á_gap) *┬á(7 -┬ácolumn) +┬á1;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat startY =┬á_gridRect.origin.y +┬á_gap
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á+ (_blockSize.height +┬á_gap) *┬árow +┬á1;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGRect blockFrame =┬áCGRectMake(startX, startY,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á _blockSize.width, _blockSize.height);
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIColor *color =┬á[_document stateAtRow:row column:column] ?
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [UIColor blackColor] :┬á[UIColor whiteColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[color setFill];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.tintColor setStroke];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIBezierPath *path =┬á[UIBezierPath bezierPathWithRect:blockFrame];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[path fill];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[path stroke];
}
```

This code uses the grid origin and the cell size and gap values set by the `calculateGridForSize:` method to figure out where each cell should be, and then draws it using the current tint color for the outline, and either black or white for the interior fill, depending on whether the cell should be filled or not. The methods that are used for drawing will be explained in [Chapter 16](#9781484202005_Ch16.xhtml).

Finally, we add a set of methods that respond to touch events by the user. Both `touchesBegan:withEvent:` and `touchesMoved:withEvent:` are standard methods that every `UIView` subclass can implement to capture touch events that happen within the viewÔÇÖs frame. WeÔÇÖll discuss these methods in detail in [Chapter 19](#9781484202005_Ch19.xhtml). Our implementation of these two methods uses two other methods weÔÇÖre adding here to calculate a grid location based on a touch location and to toggle a specific value in the document. Again, these methods use the values set by the `calculateGridForSize:` method to decide whether a touch falls within a grid cell or not. Add these four methods at the bottom of the file, just above the `@end`:

```
- (GridIndex)touchedGridIndexFromTouches:(NSSet *)touches {
┬á┬á┬á┬áGridIndex result;
┬á┬á┬á┬áresult.row =┬á-1;
┬á┬á┬á┬áresult.column =┬á-1;
┬á┬á┬á┬áUITouch *touch =┬á[touches anyObject];
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint location =┬á[touch locationInView:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (CGRectContainsPoint(_gridRect, location)) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álocation.x -= _gridRect.origin.x;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álocation.y -= _gridRect.origin.y;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áresult.column =┬á8 -┬á(location.x *┬á8.0 /┬á_gridRect.size.width);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áresult.row =┬álocation.y *┬á8.0 /┬á_gridRect.size.height;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn result;
}

- (void)toggleSelectedBlock {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (_selectedBlockIndex.row != -1 && _selectedBlockIndex.column != -1) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[_document toggleStateAtRow:_selectedBlockIndex.row
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á column:_selectedBlockIndex.column];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[[_document.undoManager prepareWithInvocationTarget:_document]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á toggleStateAtRow:_selectedBlockIndex.row column:_selectedBlockIndex.column];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setNeedsDisplay];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}

- (void)touchesBegan:(NSSet *)touches withEvent:(UIEvent *)event {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.selectedBlockIndex =┬á[self touchedGridIndexFromTouches:touches];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self toggleSelectedBlock];
}

- (void)touchesMoved:(NSSet *)touches withEvent:(UIEvent *)event {
┬á┬á┬á┬áGridIndex touched =┬á[self touchedGridIndexFromTouches:touches];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (touched.row != _selectedBlockIndex.row
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á || touched.column != _selectedBlockIndex.column) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_selectedBlockIndex =┬átouched;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self toggleSelectedBlock];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Sharp-eyed readers may have noticed that the `toggleSelectedBlock` method does something a bit special. After calling the documentÔÇÖs `toggleStateAtRow:column:` method to change the value of a particular grid point, it does something more. LetÔÇÖs take another look:

```
- (void)toggleSelectedBlock {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (_selectedBlockIndex.row != -1 && _selectedBlockIndex.column != -1) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[_document toggleStateAtRow:_selectedBlockIndex.row
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á column:_selectedBlockIndex.column];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[[_document.undoManager prepareWithInvocationTarget:_document]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á toggleStateAtRow:_selectedBlockIndex.row
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á column:_selectedBlockIndex.column];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setNeedsDisplay];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

The call to `_document.``undoManager` returns an instance of `NSUndoManager`. We havenÔÇÖt dealt with this directly anywhere else in this book, but `NSUndoManager` is the structural underpinning for the undo/redo functionality in both iOS and OS X. The idea is that anytime the user performs an action in the GUI, you use `NSUndoManager` to leave a sort of breadcrumb by ÔÇ£recordingÔÇØ a method call that will undo what the user just did. `NSUndoManager` will store that method call on a special undo stack, which can be used to backtrack through a documentÔÇÖs state whenever the user activates the systemÔÇÖs undo functionality.

The way it works is that the `prepareWithInvocationTarget:` method returns a proxy object to which you can send any message, and the message will be packed up with the target and pushed onto the undo stack. So, while it may look like youÔÇÖre calling `toggleStateAtRow:column:` twice in a row, the second time itÔÇÖs not being called but instead is just being queued up for later potential use. This kind of spectacularly dynamic behavior is an area where Objective-C really stands out in comparison to static languages such as C++, where techniques such as letting one object act as a proxy to another or packing up a method invocation for later use have no language support and are nearly impossible (and therefore many tasks, such as building undo support, can be quite tedious).

So, why are we doing this? We havenÔÇÖt been giving any thought to undo/redo issues up to this point, so why now? The reason is that registering an undoable action with the documentÔÇÖs `NSUndoManager` marks the document as ÔÇ£dirtyÔÇØ and ensures that it will be saved automatically at some point in the next few seconds. The fact that the userÔÇÖs actions are also undoable is just icing on the cake, at least in this application. In an app with a more complex document structure, allowing document-wide undo support can be hugely beneficial.

Save your changes. Now that our view class is ready to go, letÔÇÖs head back to the storyboard to configure the GUI for the detail view.

Storyboard Detailing

Select *Main.storyboard*, find the detail scene, and take a look at whatÔÇÖs there right now.

All the GUI contains is a label (ÔÇ£Detail view content goes hereÔÇØ), which is the one that contained the documentÔÇÖs description when you ran the app earlier. That label isnÔÇÖt particularly useful, so select the label in the detail view controller and press the **Delete** key to remove it.

Use the object library to find a **UIView** and drag it into the detail view. Position and size it so that it fills the entire area below the title bar (see [Figure 14-5](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig5)).

![9781484202005_Fig14-05.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-05.jpg)

[Figure 14-5](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig5). We replaced the label in the detail view with another view, centered in its containing view. The view becomes somewhat invisible while dragging, but here you can see that itÔÇÖs partly covering the dashed lines that appear when you drag it to the center of the view

Switch over to the Identity Inspector, so we can change this `UIView` instance into an instance of our custom class. In the **Custom Class** section at the top of the inspector, select the **Class** pop-up list and choose **TinyPixView**. Now open the Attributes Inspector and change the **Mode** setting to *Redraw*. This causes TinyPixView to redraw itself when its size changes. This is necessary because the position of the grid inside the view depends on the size of the view itself, which changes when the device is rotated. At this point, the view hierarchy for the Detail Scene should look like [Figure 14-6](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig6).

![9781484202005_Fig14-06.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-06.jpg)

[Figure 14-6](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig6). The detail view sceneÔÇÖs view hierarchy

Before we go on, we need to adjust the auto layout constraints for the new view. We want it to fill the available area in the detail view. So, in the Document Outline, Control-drag from TinyPixView to its parent view and release the mouse. Hold down the **Shift** key and in the pop-up, select **Leading Space to Container Margin**, **Trailing Space to Container Margin**, **Top Space to Top Layout Guide**, and **Bottom Space to Bottom Layout Guide**, and then click outside the pop-up to apply the constraints.

Now we need to wire up the custom view to our detail view controller. We havenÔÇÖt prepared an outlet for our custom view yet, but thatÔÇÖs OK since XcodeÔÇÖs drag-to-code feature will do that for us.

Activate the Assistant Editor. A text editor should slide into place alongside the GUI editor, displaying the contents of *DetailViewController.m*. If itÔÇÖs showing you anything else, use the jump bar at the top of the text editor to make *DetailViewController.m* come into view.

To make the connection, Control-drag from the **TinyPixView** icon in the Document Outline to the code, releasing the drag in the class extension at the top of the file. In the pop-up window that appears, make sure that Connection is set to *Outlet*, name the new outlet *pixView*, and click the **Connect** button.

You should see that making that connection has added this line to *DetailViewController.m*:

```
@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet TinyPixView *pixView;
```

One thing it didnÔÇÖt add, however, is any knowledge of our custom view class to the source code. LetÔÇÖs take care of that by adding this line toward the top of *DetailViewController.m*:

```
#import "DetailViewController.h"
#import "TinyPixView.h"

@interface DetailViewController ()
```

Now letÔÇÖs modify the `configureView` method. This isnÔÇÖt a standard `UIViewController` method. ItÔÇÖs just a private method that the project template included in this class as a convenient spot to put code that needs to update the view after anything changes. Since weÔÇÖre not using the description label, we delete the line that sets that. Next, we add a bit of code to pass the chosen document along to our custom view and tell it to redraw itself by calling `setNeedsDisplay`:

```
- (void)configureView
{
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Update the user interface for the detail item.

┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.detailItem) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.detailDescriptionLabel.text =┬á[self.detailItem description];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.pixView.document =┬áself.detailItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.pixView setNeedsDisplay];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Next, we need to arrange for the tint color to be applied to the `TinyPixView`. We need to do this both when the view is first loaded and whenever the tint color is changed. We know that we can get the initial tint color from the user defaults, so letÔÇÖs add a method that gets the value saved there, converts it to a `UIColor`, and applies it to the `TinyPixView`. The conversion requires the `TinyPixUtils` class that we created earlier, so first add an import for that class at the top of the file:

```
#import "DetailViewController.h"
#import "TinyPixView.h"
#import "TinyPixUtils.h"
```

Next, add this method somewhere in the body of the class:

```
- (void)updateTintColor {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDefaults *prefs =┬á[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSInteger selectedColorIndex =┬á[prefs integerForKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIColor *tintColor =┬á[TinyPixUtils getTintColorForIndex:selectedColorIndex];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.pixView .tintColor =┬átintColor;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.pixView setNeedsDisplay];
}
```

We need to call this method to set the initial tint color when the view is first loaded. We also need to call it when the tint changes. How will we know thatÔÇÖs happened? When the tint color is changed, the new value is saved in the user defaults. You can find out that something in the user defaults has changed by registering an observer for the `NSUserDefaultsDidChangeNotification` notification with the default notification center. Add the following code to the `viewDidLoad` method:

```
- (void)viewDidLoad {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewDidLoad];
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Do any additional setup after loading the view, typically from a┬ánib.
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self configureView];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateTintColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter] addObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselector:@selector(onSettingsChanged:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áname:NSUserDefaultsDidChangeNotification object:nil];
}
```

Now, when anything in the user defaults changes, the `onSettingsChanged:` method is called. When this happens, we need to set the new tint color, in case itÔÇÖs changed. Add the implementation of this method above the `@end` in the class:

```
- (void)onSettingsChanged:(NSNotification *)notification {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateTintColor];
}
```

Having added a notification observer, we have to remove it before the class is deallocated. We can do this by overriding the viewÔÇÖs `dealloc` method:

```
- (void)dealloc {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter] removeObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áname:NSUserDefaultsDidChangeNotification object:nil];
}
```

WeÔÇÖre nearly finished with this class, but we need to make one more change. Remember when we mentioned the autosaving that takes place when a document is notified that some editing has occurred, triggered by registering an undoable action? The save normally happens within about 10 seconds after the edit occurs. Like the other saving and loading procedures we described earlier in this chapter, it happens in a background thread, so that normally the user wonÔÇÖt even notice. However, that works only as long as the document is still around.

With our current setup, thereÔÇÖs a risk that when the user hits the **Back** button to go back to the master list, the document instance will be deallocated without any save operation occurring, and the userÔÇÖs latest changes will be lost. To make sure this doesnÔÇÖt happen, we need to add some code to the `viewWillDisappear:` method to close the document as soon as the user navigates away from the detail view. Closing a document causes it to be automatically saved, and again, the saving occurs on a background thread. In this particular case, we donÔÇÖt need to do anything when the save is done, so we pass in `nil` instead of a block:

Add this `viewWillDisappear:` method:

```
- (void)viewWillDisappear:(BOOL)animated {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewWillDisappear:animated];
┬á┬á┬á┬áUIDocument *doc =┬áself.detailItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[doc closeWithCompletionHandler:nil];
}
```

And with that, this version of our first truly document-based app is ready to try out! Fire it up and bask in the glory. You can create new documents, edit them, flip back to the list, and then select another document (or the same document), and it all just works. Experiment with changing the tint color and verify that it is properly saved and restored when you stop and restart the app. If you open the Xcode console while doing this, youÔÇÖll see some output each time a document is loaded or saved. Using the autosaving system, you donÔÇÖt have direct control over just when saves occur (except for when closing a document), but it can be interesting to watch the logs just to get a feel for when they happen.

Adding iCloud Support

You now have a fully working document-based app, but weÔÇÖre not going to stop here. We promised you iCloud support in this chapter, and itÔÇÖs time to deliver!

Modifying TinyPix to work with iCloud is pretty straightforward. Considering all thatÔÇÖs happening behind the scenes, this requires a surprisingly small number of changes. WeÔÇÖll need to make some revisions to the method that loads the list of available files and the method that specifies the URL for loading a new file, but thatÔÇÖs about it.

Apart from the code changes, we will also need to deal with some additional administrative details. Apple allows an app to save to iCloud only if it contains an embedded provisioning profile that is configured to allow iCloud usage. This means that to add the iCloud support to our app, you must have a paid iOS developer membership and have installed your developer certificate. It also works only with actual devices, not the simulator, so youÔÇÖll need to have at least one iOS device registered with iCloud to run the new iCloud-backed TinyPix. With two devices, youÔÇÖll have even more fun, as you can see how changes made on one device propagate to the other.

Creating a Provisioning Profile

First, you need to create an iCloud-enabled provisioning profile for TinyPix. This used to require a lot of convoluted steps on AppleÔÇÖs developer web site, but nowadays Xcode makes it easy. In the Project Navigator, select the **TinyPix** item at the top, and then click the **Capabilities** tab in the editing area. You should see something like whatÔÇÖs shown in [Figure 14-7](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig7).

![9781484202005_Fig14-07.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-07.jpg)

[Figure 14-7](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig7). XcodeÔÇÖs presentation of easily configurable app technologies and services

The list of capabilities shown in [Figure 14-7](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig7) can all be configured directly in Xcode, all without needing to go to a web site, create and download provisioning profiles, and so on. Before you can do this, you need to give your app a unique App ID. If you used the version of the project thatÔÇÖs in the source code download, the App ID is `com.apress.BID`. This App ID is already registered, so you wonÔÇÖt be able to use it. Select the **General** tab and use a different prefix in the **Bundle Identifier** field. To change the App ID to `com.myCo`, for example, you would set the Bundle Identifier as shown in [Figure 14-8](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig8).

![9781484202005_Fig14-08.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-08.jpg)

[Figure 14-8](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig8). Changing the applicationÔÇÖs bundle ID

Of course, you should use a value thatÔÇÖs unique to you rather than `com.myCo`. Now switch back to the **Capabilities** tab. For TinyPix, we want to enable iCloud, the first capability listed, so click the disclosure triangle next to the cloud icon. Here youÔÇÖll see some information about what this capability is for. Click the switch at the right to turn it on. Xcode will then communicate with AppleÔÇÖs servers to configure the provisioning profile for this app. This will require you to log in with your Apple ID, and it obviously requires you to be connected to the Internet. After itÔÇÖs enabled, click to turn on the **Key-value storage** and **iCloud Documents** check boxes, as shown in [Figure 14-9](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig9).

![9781484202005_Fig14-09.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig14-09.jpg)

[Figure 14-9](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#_Fig9). The app is now configured to use iCloud. This simple configuration let us remove several pages from this chapter, which probably ends up saving the life of a tree or two. Thanks, Apple!

YouÔÇÖre finished! Your app now has the necessary permissions to access iCloud from your code. The rest is a simple matter of programming.

How to Query

Select *MasterViewController.m* so that we can start making changes for iCloud. The biggest change is going to be the way we look for available documents. In the first version of TinyPix, we used `NSFileManager` to see whatÔÇÖs available on the local file system. This time, weÔÇÖre going to do things a little differently. Here, we will fire up a special sort of query to look for documents.

Start by adding a pair of properties in the class extension: one to hold a pointer to an ongoing query and the other to hold the list of all the documents the query finds.

```
@interface MasterViewController ()

@property (weak, nonatomic) IBOutlet UISegmentedControl *colorControl;
@property (strong, nonatomic) NSArray *documentFilenames;
@property (strong, nonatomic) TinyPixDocument *chosenDocument;
@property (strong, nonatomic) NSMetadataQuery *query;
@property (strong, nonatomic) NSMutableArray *documentURLs;

@end
```

Now, letÔÇÖs look at the new file-listing method. Remove the entire `reloadFiles` method and replace it with this:

```
- (void)reloadFiles {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSFileManager *fileManager =┬á[NSFileManager defaultManager];
┬á┬á┬á┬á// passing nil is OK here, matches first entitlement
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *cloudURL =┬á[fileManager URLForUbiquityContainerIdentifier:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"got cloudURL %@", cloudURL);┬á┬á// returns nil in simulator
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (cloudURL != nil) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.query =┬á[[NSMetadataQuery alloc] init];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_query.predicate =┬á[NSPredicate predicateWithFormat:@"%K like '*.tinypix'",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSMetadataItemFSNameKey];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_query.searchScopes =┬á[NSArray arrayWithObject:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á NSMetadataQueryUbiquitousDocumentsScope];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áaddObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselector:@selector(updateUbiquitousDocuments:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áname:NSMetadataQueryDidFinishGatheringNotification
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áobject:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áaddObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselector:@selector(updateUbiquitousDocuments:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áname:NSMetadataQueryDidUpdateNotification
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áobject:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[_query startQuery];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

There are some new things here that are definitely worth mentioning. The first is seen in this line:

```
NSURL *cloudURL =┬á[fileManager URLForUbiquityContainerIdentifier:nil];
```

ThatÔÇÖs a mouthful, for sure. Ubiquity? What are we talking about here? When it comes to iCloud, a lot of AppleÔÇÖs terminology for identifying resources in iCloud storage includes words like ÔÇ£ubiquityÔÇØ and ÔÇ£ubiquitousÔÇØ to indicate that something is omnipresentÔÇöaccessible from any device using the same iCloud login credentials.

In this case, weÔÇÖre asking the file manager to give us a base URL that will let us access the iCloud directory associated with a particular **container identifier**. A container identifier is normally a string containing your companyÔÇÖs unique bundle seed ID and the application identifier. The container identifier is used to pick one of the iCloud entitlements contained within your app. Passing `nil` here is a shortcut that just means ÔÇ£give me the first one in the list.ÔÇØ Since our app contains only one checked item in that list (which you can see listed under ÔÇ£ContainersÔÇØ at the bottom of [Figure 14-9](#9781484202005_Ch14.xhtml#Fig9)), that shortcut suits our needs perfectly.

After that, we create and configure an instance of `NSMetadataQuery`:

```
self.query =┬á[[NSMetadataQuery alloc] init];
_query.predicate =┬á[NSPredicate predicateWithFormat:@"%K like '*.tinypix'",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSMetadataItemFSNameKey];
_query.searchScopes =┬á[NSArray arrayWithObject:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á NSMetadataQueryUbiquitousDocumentsScope];
```

The `NSMetaDataQuery` class was originally written for use with the Spotlight search facility on OS X, but itÔÇÖs now doing extra duty as a way to let iOS apps search iCloud directories. We give the query a predicate, which limits its search results to include only those with the correct sort of file name, and we give it a search scope that limits it to look just within the *Documents* folder in the appÔÇÖs iCloud storage. Next, we set up some notifications to let us know when the query is complete, and then we initiate the query:

```
[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áaddObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselector:@selector(updateUbiquitousDocuments:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áname:NSMetadataQueryDidFinishGatheringNotification
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áobject:nil];
[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áaddObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselector:@selector(updateUbiquitousDocuments:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áname:NSMetadataQueryDidUpdateNotification
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áobject:nil];
[_query startQuery];
```

Now we need to implement the method that those notifications call when the query is done. Add this method just below the `reloadFiles` method:

```
- (void)updateUbiquitousDocuments:(NSNotification *)notification {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.documentURLs =┬á[NSMutableArray array];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.documentFilenames =┬á[NSMutableArray array];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSLog(@"updateUbiquitousDocuments, results =┬á%@", self.query.results);
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSArray *results =┬á[self.query.results sortedArrayUsingComparator:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á^NSComparisonResult(id obj1, id obj2) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSMetadataItem *item1 =┬áobj1;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSMetadataItem *item2 =┬áobj2;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [[item2 valueForAttribute:NSMetadataItemFSCreationDateKey]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácompare:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[item1 valueForAttribute:NSMetadataItemFSCreationDateKey]];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}];

┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (NSMetadataItem *item in results) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *url =┬á[item valueForAttribute:NSMetadataItemURLKey];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.documentURLs addObject:url];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[(NSMutableArray *)_documentFilenames addObject:[url lastPathComponent]];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.tableView reloadData];
}
```

The queryÔÇÖs results contain a list of `NSMetadataItem` objects, from which we can get items like file URLs and creation dates. We use this to sort the items by date, and then grab all the URLs for later use.

Save Where?

The next change is to the `urlForFilename:` method, which once again is completely different. Here weÔÇÖre using a ubiquitous URL to create a full path URL for a given file name. We insert `"Documents"` in the generated path as well, to make sure weÔÇÖre using the appÔÇÖs *Documents* directory. Delete the old method and replace it with this new one:

```
- (NSURL *)urlForFilename:(NSString *)filename {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// be sure to insert "Documents" into the path
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *baseURL =┬á[[NSFileManager defaultManager]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áURLForUbiquityContainerIdentifier:nil];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *pathURL =┬á[baseURL URLByAppendingPathComponent:@"Documents"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSURL *destinationURL =┬á[pathURL URLByAppendingPathComponent:filename];
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn destinationURL;
}
```

Now, build and run your app on an actual iOS device (not the simulator). If youÔÇÖve run the previous version of the app on that device, youÔÇÖll find that any TinyPix masterpieces you created earlier are now nowhere to be seen. This new version ignores the local *Documents* directory for the app and relies completely on iCloud. However, you should be able to create new documents and find that they stick around after quitting and restarting the app. Moreover, you can even delete the TinyPix app from your device entirely, run it again from Xcode, and find that all your iCloud-saved documents are available at once. If you have an additional iOS device configured with the same iCloud user, use Xcode to run the app on that device, and youÔÇÖll see all the same documents appear there, as well! ItÔÇÖs pretty sweet. You can also find these documents in the iCloud section of your iOS deviceÔÇÖs Settings app (look under **Storage** ![image](images/arrow.jpg) **Manage Storage** ![image](images/arrow.jpg) **TinyPix**), as well as the iCloud section of your MacÔÇÖs System Preferences app if youÔÇÖre running OS X 10.8 or later.

Storing Preferences on iCloud

We can ÔÇ£cloudifyÔÇØ one more piece of functionality with just a bit of effort. iOSÔÇÖs iCloud support includes a class called `NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore`, which works a lot like `NSUserDefaults`; however, its keys and values are stored in the cloud. This is great for application preferences, login tokens, and anything else that doesnÔÇÖt belong in a document, but could be useful when shared among all of a userÔÇÖs devices.

In TinyPix, weÔÇÖll use this feature to store the userÔÇÖs preferred highlight color. That way, instead of needing to be configured on each device, the user sets the color once, and it shows up everywhere. HereÔÇÖs the plan of action:

*   Whenever the user changes the tint color, weÔÇÖll save the new value in `NSUserDefaults` and weÔÇÖll also save it in the `NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore`, which will make it available to instances of the application on other devices.
*   WeÔÇÖll register to be notified of changes in the `NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore`. When weÔÇÖre notified of a change, weÔÇÖll get the new tint color value. At this point, we need to update the segmented control and the tint color used by the master view controller and the drawing color in the detail view controller. Rather than do this directly, weÔÇÖll just save the new tint color in `NSUserDefaults`. Changing `NSUserDefaults` causes a notification to be generated. The detail view controller is already observing this notification, so it will update itself automatically. WeÔÇÖre going to make some small changes to the master view controller so that it does the same thing.

ItÔÇÖs important to be aware that updates to `NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore` do not propagate immediately to other devices, and, in fact, if a device is not connected to iCloud for any reason, it wonÔÇÖt see the update until it next connects. So donÔÇÖt expect changes to be seen immediately.

LetÔÇÖs start by registering to receive change notifications from the iCloud key-value store. Open *AppDelegate.m* and add the following code to the `application:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:` method:

```
- (BOOL)application:(UIApplication *)application didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:(NSDictionary *)launchOptions {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// Override point for customization after application launch.
┬á┬á┬á┬áUISplitViewController *splitViewController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á (UISplitViewController *)self.window.rootViewController;
┬á┬á┬á┬áUINavigationController *navigationController =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [splitViewController.viewControllers lastObject];
┬á┬á┬á┬ánavigationController.topViewController.navigationItem.leftBarButtonItem =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á splitViewController.displayModeButtonItem;
┬á┬á┬á┬ásplitViewController.delegate =┬áself;

┬á┬á┬á┬á// Register for notification of iCloud key-value changes
┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter] addObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á selector:@selector(iCloudKeysChanged:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á name:NSUbiquitousKeyValueStoreDidChangeExternallyNotification
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á object:nil];

┬á┬á┬á┬á// Start iCloud key-value updates
┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore defaultStore] synchronize];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateUserDefaultsFromICloud];

┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
}
```

The first new line of code arranges for the application delegateÔÇÖs `iCloudKeysChanged:` method to be called when an `NSUbiquitousKeyValueStoreDidChangeExternallyNotification` occursÔÇöthat is, when iCloud notifies a change in any of the applicationÔÇÖs key/value pairs. The `synchronize` method causes local changes to the `NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore` to be written to iCloud in the background and notification of remote updates to start. The `updateUserDefaultsFromICloud` method, which youÔÇÖll see shortly, gets the current state of the selected tint color from the iCloud key-value store, if itÔÇÖs set, and stores it in the local user defaults, so that it will be used immediately.

Next, add the implementation of the `iCloudKeysChanged:` and `updateUserDefaultsFromCloud` methods:

```
- (void)iCloudKeysChanged:(NSNotification *)notification {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateUserDefaultsFromICloud];
}

- (void)updateUserDefaultsFromICloud {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSDictionary *values =┬á[[NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore defaultStore] dictionaryRepresentation];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([values valueForKey:@"selectedColorIndex"] != nil) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUInteger selectedColorIndex =┬á(NSUInteger)[[NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore defaultStore] longLongForKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDefaults *prefs =┬á[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[prefs setInteger:selectedColorIndex forKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[prefs synchronize];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

When a notification occurs, we use the `longLongForKey:` method to get the new selected tint color index from the key store. The API is very similar to that of `NSUserDefaults`, but there is no method to store an integer value, so we treat the tint color index as a `long long` instead. Once we have the value, we simply copy it to the `NSUserDefaults` and synchronize the change, so that a notification is generated. We already know that the detail view controller will update itself when it receives this notification. Next, we need to change the master view controller so that it does the same. Back in *MasterViewController.m*, start by registering the controller to be notified of `NSUserDefaults` changes in its `viewDidLoad` method:

```
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self reloadFiles];

┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSNotificationCenter defaultCenter] addObserver:self
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á selector:@selector(onSettingsChanged:)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á name:NSUserDefaultsDidChangeNotification object:nil];
}
```

Next, add the `onSettingsChanged:` method:

```
- (void)onSettingsChanged:(NSNotification *)notification {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDefaults *prefs =┬á[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSInteger selectedColorIndex =┬á[prefs integerForKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setTintColorForIndex:selectedColorIndex];
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.colorControl.selectedSegmentIndex =┬áselectedColorIndex;
}
```

This method updates the tint color of the segmented control using the same method thatÔÇÖs called when the user taps one of its segments, but it gets the color index from `NSUserDefaults` instead of from the control.

Finally, when the user changes the tint color, we need to save the new index in the iCloud key-value store. Make the following changes to the `chooseColor:` method to take care of this:

```
- (IBAction)chooseColor:(id)sender {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSInteger selectedColorIndex =┬á[(UISegmentedControl *)sender
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áselectedSegmentIndex];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self setTintColorForIndex:selectedColorIndex];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUserDefaults *prefs =┬á[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[prefs setInteger:selectedColorIndex forKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[prefs synchronize];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore defaultStore]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ásetLongLong:selectedColorIndex
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áforKey:@"selectedColorIndex"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[[NSUbiquitousKeyValueStore defaultStore] synchronize];
}
```

ThatÔÇÖs it! You can now run the app on multiple devices configured for the same iCloud user and will see that setting the color on one device results in the new color appearing on the other device soon afterward. Piece of cake!

What We DidnÔÇÖt Cover

We now have the basics of an iCloud-enabled, document-based application up and running, but there are a few more issues that you may want to consider. WeÔÇÖre not going to cover these topics in this book; but if youÔÇÖre serious about making a great iCloud-based app, youÔÇÖll want to think about these areas:

*   Documents stored in iCloud are prone to conflicts. What happens if you edit the same TinyPix file on several devices at once? Fortunately, Apple has already thought of this and provides some ways to deal with these conflicts in your app. ItÔÇÖs up to you to decide whether you want to ignore conflicts, try to fix them automatically, or ask the user to help sort out the problem. For full details, search for a document titled ÔÇ£Resolving Document Version ConflictsÔÇØ in the Xcode documentation viewer.
*   Apple recommends that you design your application to work in a completely offline mode in case the user isnÔÇÖt using iCloud for some reason. It also recommends that you provide a way for a user to move files between iCloud storage and local storage. Sadly, Apple doesnÔÇÖt provide or suggest any standard GUI for helping a user manage this, and current apps that provide this functionality, such as AppleÔÇÖs iWork apps, donÔÇÖt seem to handle it in a particularly user-friendly way. See AppleÔÇÖs ÔÇ£Managing the Life Cycle of a DocumentÔÇØ in the Xcode documentation for more on this.
*   Apple supports using iCloud for Core Data storage and even provides a class called `UIManagedDocument` that you can subclass if you want to make that work. See the `UIManagedDocument` class reference for more information. This architecture is a lot more complex and problematic than normal iCloud document storage. Apple has taken steps to improve things in recent versions of iOS, but itÔÇÖs still not perfectly smooth, so look before you leap.

WhatÔÇÖs up next? In [Chapter 15](#9781484202005_Ch15.xhtml), weÔÇÖll take you through the process of making sure your apps work properly in a multithreaded, multitasking environment.

Chapter┬á┬á┬á┬á15

Grand Central Dispatch, Background Processing, and You

If youÔÇÖve ever tried your hand at multithreaded programming, in any environment, chances are youÔÇÖve come away from the experience with a feeling of dread, terror, or worse. Fortunately, technology marches on, and Apple has come up with a new approach that makes multithreaded programming much easier. This approach is called **Grand Central Dispatch**, and weÔÇÖll get you started using it in this chapter.
