Here, the `SKScene` node is selected, so the SKNode Inspector is displaying its properties. Below the inspector, in the bottom right, is the usual Xcode Object Library, which is automatically filtered to show only the types of objects you can add to a Sprite Kit scene. You design your scene by dragging objects from here and dropping them onto the editor.

**Tip**┬á┬áYou may be wondering why the method used to load the scene is from the scene file called `nodeWithFileNamed:` and not `sceneWithFileNamed:`. Although the template code only uses this method to load the initial scene, it can actually load any Sprite Kit node, which means you can archive a node to a file and load it back later. In fact, you can use the Level Designer to create a scene consisting of a single node that you can save and load into the scene graph later. This allows you to design complex nodes without having to write any code.

Now letÔÇÖs go back and finish up our discussion of the `viewDidLoad` method.

```
scene.scaleMode =┬áSKSceneScaleModeAspectFill;

// Present the scene.
[skView presentScene:scene];
```

These two lines of code set the sceneÔÇÖs scale mode and make the scene visible. LetÔÇÖs talk about those two things in reverse order. In order for a scene and its content to be visible and active, it must be presented by an `SKView`. To present a scene, you call the `SKView`ÔÇÖs `presentScene:` method. An `SKView` can display only one scene at a time, so calling this method when thereÔÇÖs already a presented scene causes the new scene to immediately replace the old one. If you are switching from one scene to another, you should probably prefer to use the `presentScene:transition:` method, which animates the scene change. YouÔÇÖll see examples of this later in the chapter. In this case, since we are making the initial scene visible, there is nothing to transition from, so itÔÇÖs acceptable to use the `presentScene:` method.

Now letÔÇÖs talk about the sceneÔÇÖs `scaleMode` property. If you look back at [Figure 17-2](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig2), youÔÇÖll see that the default scene in the Level Designer is 1024 points wide and 768 points highÔÇöthe same as the size of an iPad screen. ThatÔÇÖs all well and good if you plan to run your game only in landscape mode on an iPad, but what about portrait mode, or other screen sizes, like on the iPhone? How should you adapt the scene for the size of the screen that your application is running on? There is no simple answer to that question. There are four different ways to adjust the size of the scene when itÔÇÖs presented in an `SKView`, corresponding to the four values of the `SKSceneScaleMode` enumeration. To see what each scale mode does, letÔÇÖs create another Sprite Kit project and experiment with it. Using the same steps as before, create a Sprite Kit project, call it *ResizeModes*, and select the *GameScene.sks* file in the Project Navigator. At this point, your Xcode window should look like [Figure 17-2](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig2).

In the Object Library, locate a *Label* node and drag it into the center of the scene. In the SKNode Inspector, use the **Text**, field to change the labelÔÇÖs text to *Center*. Drag another label to the bottom left of the scene, placing it carefully so that itÔÇÖs exactly in the corner of the scene. Change its `text` property to *Bottom Left*. Drag a third label to the top right of the scene and change its text to *Top Right*. Drag a couple more labels to the top and bottom of the scene and name them *Top* and *Bottom*, respectively. You can change the colors and fonts associated with the labels to make the text more visible, if necessary. When youÔÇÖre done, you should have something like the scene shown in [Figure 17-3](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig3).

![9781484202005_Fig17-03.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-03.jpg)

[Figure 17-3](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig3). Using the Sprite Kit Level Designer to add nodes to a scene

**Tip**┬á┬áIf you canÔÇÖt see all of the scene in the Editor area, you can use the -/=/+ buttons at the bottom right of the Editor to zoom out until you have enough of the scene in view to work comfortably with it.

Select *GameScene.m* in the Project Navigator and delete the `didMoveToView:` method. This method contains the code that adds the ÔÇ£Hello, WorldÔÇØ label to the scene, which we donÔÇÖt need. Next, select *GameViewController.m* and locate the line of code in the `viewDidLoad` method that sets the `scaleMode` of the `SKScene` object. As you can see, itÔÇÖs initially set to `SKSceneScaleModeAspectFill`. Run the application on an iPhone simulator (or device) with this scale mode set, and then edit the code and run it three more times, using the values `SKSceneScaleModeAspectFit`, `SKSceneScaleModeFill`, and `SKSceneScaleModeResizeFill`. The results are shown in [Figure 17-4](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig4).

![9781484202005_Fig17-04.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-04.jpg)

[Figure 17-4](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig4). Comparing the four scene rescale modes

HereÔÇÖs what these modes do:

*   `SKSceneScaleModeAspectFill` resizes the scene so that it fills the screen while preserving its aspect (width-to-height ratio). As you can see in [Figure 17-4](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig4), this mode ensures that every pixel of the `SKView` is covered, but loses part of the sceneÔÇöin this case, the scene has been cropped on the left and right. The content of the scene is also scaled, so the text is smaller than in the original scene, but its position relative to the scene is preserved.
*   `SKSceneScaleModeAspectFit` also preserves the sceneÔÇÖs aspect ratio, but ensures that the whole scene is visible. The result is a letter-box view, with parts of the `SKView` visible above and below the scene content.
*   `SKSceneScaleModeFill` scales the scene along both axes so that it exactly fits the view. This ensures that everything in the scene is visible, but since the aspect ratio of the original scene is not preserved, there may be unacceptable distortion of the content. Here, you can see that the text has been horizontally compressed.
*   Finally, `SKSceneScaleModeResizeFill` places the bottom-left corner of the scene in the bottom-left corner of the view and leaves it at its original size.

Which of these rescale modes is best for you depends on the needs of your application. If none of them work, there are two other possibilities. First, you can elect to support a fixed set of screen sizes, create an individual design tailored to each of them, store it in its own *.sks* file, and load the scene from the correct file when itÔÇÖs needed. Secondly, you can simply create the scene in code, make it the same size as the `SKView` in which itÔÇÖs being presented, and populate it with nodes programmatically. This only works if your game doesnÔÇÖt depend on the exact relative positions of its elements. To illustrate how this approach works, weÔÇÖll use it for the TextShooter application.

Initial Scene Customization

Open the TextShooter project and select the `GameScene` class. We donÔÇÖt need most of the code that the Xcode template generated for us, so letÔÇÖs remove it. First, delete the entire `didMoveToView:` method. This method is called whenever the scene is presented in an `SKView` and it is typically used to make last-minute changes to the scene before it becomes visible. Next, take away most of the `touchesBegan:withEvent:` method, leaving just the `for` loop and the first line of code it contains. At this point, your `GameScene` class should look like the following (the compiler will warn that `location` is an unused variableÔÇödonÔÇÖt worry about that, because weÔÇÖll fix it later):

```
@implementation GameScene

-(void)touchesBegan:(NSSet *)touches withEvent:(UIEvent *)event {
┬á┬á┬á┬á/* Called when a┬átouch begins */

┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (UITouch *touch in touches) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint location =┬á[touch locationInNode:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}

-(void)update:(CFTimeInterval)currentTime {
┬á┬á┬á┬á/* Called before each frame is rendered */
}

@end
```

Since weÔÇÖre not going to load our scene from *GameScene.sks*, we need a method that will create a scene for us, with some initial content. WeÔÇÖll also need to add properties for the current game-level number, the number of lives the player has, and a flag to let us know whether the level is finished. Add the following bold lines to *GameScene.h*:

```
@interface GameScene :┬áSKScene

@property (assign, nonatomic) NSUInteger levelNumber;
@property (assign, nonatomic) NSUInteger playerLives;
@property (assign, nonatomic) BOOL finished;

+ (instancetype)sceneWithSize:(CGSize)size levelNumber:(NSUInteger)levelNumber;
- (instancetype)initWithSize:(CGSize)size levelNumber:(NSUInteger)levelNumber;

@end
```

Now switch over to *GameScene.m*, where weÔÇÖll implement the two new methods that we just declared. Add the following code in the implementation section of the file:

```
+ (instancetype)sceneWithSize:(CGSize)size levelNumber:(NSUInteger)levelNumber {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [[self alloc] initWithSize:size levelNumber:levelNumber];
}

- (instancetype)initWithSize:(CGSize)size {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn [self initWithSize:size levelNumber:1];
}

- (instancetype)initWithSize:(CGSize)size levelNumber:(NSUInteger)levelNumber {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super initWithSize:size]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_levelNumber =┬álevelNumber;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_playerLives =┬á5;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.backgroundColor =┬á[SKColor whiteColor];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *lives =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.fontSize =┬á16;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.fontColor =┬á[SKColor blackColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.name =┬á@"LivesLabel";
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.text =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Lives: %lu",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(unsigned long)_playerLives];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.verticalAlignmentMode =┬áSKLabelVerticalAlignmentModeTop;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.horizontalAlignmentMode =┬áSKLabelHorizontalAlignmentModeRight;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álives.position =┬áCGPointMake(self.frame.size.width,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á self.frame.size.height);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:lives];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *level =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.fontSize =┬á16;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.fontColor =┬á[SKColor blackColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.name =┬á@"LevelLabel";
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.text =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Level: %lu",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(unsigned long)_levelNumber];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.verticalAlignmentMode =┬áSKLabelVerticalAlignmentModeTop;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.horizontalAlignmentMode =┬áSKLabelHorizontalAlignmentModeLeft;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevel.position =┬áCGPointMake(0, self.frame.size.height);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:level];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

The first method, `sceneWithSize:levelNumber:`, gives us a factory method that will work as a shorthand for creating a level and setting its level number at once. In the second method, `initWithSize:`, we override the classÔÇÖs default initializer, passing control to the third method (and passing along a default value for the level number). That third method in turn calls the designated initializer from its superclassÔÇÖs implementation. This may seem like a roundabout way of doing things, but itÔÇÖs a common pattern when you want to add new initializers to a class while still using the classÔÇÖs designated initializer.

The third method we added, `initWithSize:levelNumber:`, is where we set up the basic configuration of our level scene. First, we set the values of a couple of instance variables from the parameters that were passed in. Second, we set the sceneÔÇÖs background color. Note that weÔÇÖre using a class called `SKColor` instead of `UIColor` here. In fact, `SKColor` isnÔÇÖt really a class at all; itÔÇÖs a sort of alias that can be used in place of either `UIColor` for an iOS app or `NSColor` for an OS X app. This allows us to port games between iOS and OS X a little more easily.

After that, we create two instances of a class called `SKLabelNode`. This is a handy class that works somewhat like a `UILabel`, allowing us to add some text to the scene and letting us choose a font, set a text value, and specify some alignments. We create one label for displaying the number of lives at the upper right of the screen and another that will show the level number at the upper left of the screen. Look closely at the code that we use to position these labels. Here is the code that sets the position of the lives label:

```
lives.position =┬áCGPointMake(self.frame.size.width,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á self.frame.size.height);
```

If you think about the points weÔÇÖre passing in as the position for this label, you may be surprised to see that weÔÇÖre passing in the sceneÔÇÖs height. In UIKit, positioning anything at the height of a `UIView` would put it at the bottom of that view; but in Scene Kit, the y axis is flippedÔÇöthe coordinate origin is at the bottom left of the scene and the y axis points upward. As a result, the maximum value of the sceneÔÇÖs height is a position at the top of the screen instead. What about the labelÔÇÖs x coordinate? WeÔÇÖre setting that to be the width of the view. If you did that with a `UIView`, the view would be positioned just off the right side of the screen. That doesnÔÇÖt happen here, because we also did this:

```
lives.horizontalAlignmentMode =┬áSKLabelHorizontalAlignmentModeRight;
```

Setting the `horizontalAlignmentMode` property of the `SKLabelNode` to `SKLabelHorizontalAlignmentModeRight` moves the point of the label node thatÔÇÖs used to position it (itÔÇÖs actually a property called `position`) to the right of the text. Since we want the text to be right justified on the screen, we therefore need to set the x coordinate of the `position` property to be the width of the scene. By contrast, the text in the `level` label is left-aligned and we position it at the left edge of the scene by setting its x coordinate to zero:

```
level.horizontalAlignmentMode =┬áSKLabelHorizontalAlignmentModeLeft;
level.position =┬áCGPointMake(0, self.frame.size.height);
```

YouÔÇÖll also see that we gave each label a `name`. This works similar to a tag or identifier in other parts of UIKit, and it will let us retrieve those labels later by asking for them by name.

Now select *GameViewController.m* and make the following changes to the `viewDidLoad` method:

```
- (void)viewDidLoad
{
┬á┬á┬á┬á[super viewDidLoad];

┬á┬á┬á┬á// Configure the view.
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKView *┬áskView =┬á(SKView *)self.view;
┬á┬á┬á┬áskView.showsFPS =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬áskView.showsNodeCount =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬áskView.ignoresSiblingOrder =┬áYES;

┬á┬á┬á┬á// Create and configure the scene.
┬á┬á┬á┬áGameScene *scene =┬á[GameScene nodeWithFileNamed:@"GameScene"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áscene.scaleMode =┬áSKSceneScaleModeAspectFill;
┬á┬á┬á┬áGameScene *scene =┬á[GameScene sceneWithSize:self.view.frame.size
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevelNumber:1];

┬á┬á┬á┬á// Present the scene.
┬á┬á┬á┬á[skView presentScene:scene];
}
```

Instead of loading the scene from the scene file, weÔÇÖre using the `sceneWithSize:levelNumber:` method that we just added to `GameScene` to create and initialize the scene and make it the same size as the `SKView`. Since the view and scene are the same size, there is no longer any need to set the sceneÔÇÖs `scaleMode` property, so we can remove the line of code that does that.

At the bottom of *GameViewController.m*, youÔÇÖll see the following method that the template included for us:

```
- (BOOL)prefersStatusBarHidden {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
}
```

Returning `YES` from this method makes the iOS status bar disappear while our game is running, which is usually what you want for action games like this. Now run the game and youÔÇÖll see that we have a very basic structure in place, as shown in [Figure 17-5](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig5).

![9781484202005_Fig17-05.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-05.jpg)

[Figure 17-5](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig5). Our game doesnÔÇÖt have much fun factor right now, but at least it has a high frame rate!

**Tip**┬á┬áThe node count and frame rate at the bottom right of the scene are useful for debugging, but you donÔÇÖt want them to be there when you release your game! You can switch them off by setting the `showsFPS` and `showsNodeCount` properties of the `SKView` to `NO` in the `viewDidLoad` method of `GameViewController`. There are some other `SKView` properties that let you get more debugging informationÔÇörefer to the API documentation for the details.

Player Movement

Now itÔÇÖs time to add a little interactivity. WeÔÇÖre going to make a new class that represents a player. It will know how to draw itself using internal components, as well as how to move to a new location in a nicely animated way. Next, weÔÇÖll insert an instance of the new class into the scene and write some code to let the player move the object around by touching the screen.

Every object thatÔÇÖs going to be part of our scene must be a subclass of `SKNode`. Thus, youÔÇÖll use XcodeÔÇÖs **File** menu to create a new Cocoa Touch class named `PlayerNode` thatÔÇÖs a subclass of `SKNode`. In the nearly empty *PlayerNode.m* file thatÔÇÖs created, add the following methods:

```
- (instancetype)init {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super init]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.name =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Player %p", self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self initNodeGraph];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}

- (void)initNodeGraph {
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *label =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.fontColor =┬á[SKColor darkGrayColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.fontSize =┬á40;
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.text =┬á@"v";
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.zRotation =┬áM_PI;
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.name =┬á@"label";

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:label];

}
```

Our `PlayerNode` doesnÔÇÖt display anything itself, because a plain `SKNode` has no way to do any drawing of its own. Instead, the `init` method sets up a subnode that will do the actual drawing. This subnode is another instance of `SKLabelNode`, just like the one we created for displaying the level number and the number of lives remaining. `SKLabelNode` is a subclass of `SKNode` that *does* know how to draw itself. Another such subclass is `SKSpriteNode`. WeÔÇÖre not setting a position for the label, which means that its position is coordinate (0, 0). Just like views, each `SKNode` lives in a coordinate system that is inherited from its parent object. Giving this node a zero position means that it will appear on-screen at the `PlayerNode` instanceÔÇÖs position. Any non-zero values would effectively be an offset from that point.

We also set a rotation value for the label, so that the lowercase letter ÔÇ£vÔÇØ it contains will be shown upside-down. The name of the rotation property, `zRotation`, may seem a bit surprising; however, it simply refers to the z axis of the coordinate space in use with Sprite Kit. You only see the x and y axes on screen, but the z axis is useful for ordering items for display purposes, as well as for rotating things around. The values assigned to `zRotation` need to be in radians instead of degrees, so we assign the value `M_PI`, which is equivalent to the mathematical value *¤Ç*. Since *¤Ç* radians are equal to 180┬░, this is just what we want.

Adding the Player to the Scene

Now switch back to *GameScene.m*. Here, weÔÇÖre going to add an instance of `PlayerNode` to the scene. Start off by importing the new classÔÇÖs header and adding a property inside a new class extension:

```
#import "GameScene.h"
#import "PlayerNode.h"

@interface GameScene ()

@property (strong, nonatomic) PlayerNode *playerNode;

@end
```

Continue by adding the following bold code near the end of the `initWithSize:levelNumber:` method. Be sure to put it before the `return self` and before the right curly brace above it:

```
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:level];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_playerNode =┬á[PlayerNode node];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á_playerNode.position =┬áCGPointMake(CGRectGetMidX(self.frame),
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á CGRectGetHeight(self.frame) *┬á0.1);

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:_playerNode];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

If you build and run the app now, you should see that the player appears near the lower middle of the screen, as shown in [Figure 17-6](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig6).

![9781484202005_Fig17-06.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-06.jpg)

[Figure 17-6](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig6). An upside-down ÔÇ£vÔÇØ to the rescue!

Handling Touches: Player Movement

Next, weÔÇÖre going to put some logic back into the `touchesBegan:withEvent:` method, which we earlier left nearly empty. Insert the bold lines shown here in *GameScene.m* (youÔÇÖll get a compiler error when you add this codeÔÇöweÔÇÖll fix it shortly):

```
- (void)touchesBegan:(NSSet *)touches withEvent:(UIEvent *)event {
┬á┬á┬á┬á/* Called when a┬átouch begins */

┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (UITouch *touch in touches) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á CGPoint location =┬á[touch locationInNode:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (location.y < CGRectGetHeight(self.frame) *┬á0.2 )┬á{
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint target =┬áCGPointMake(location.x,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á self.playerNode.position.y);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.playerNode moveToward:target];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

The preceding snippet uses any touch location in the lower fifth of the screen as the basis of a new location toward which you want the player node to move. It also tells the player node to move toward it. The compiler complains because we havenÔÇÖt defined the player nodeÔÇÖs `moveToward:` method yet. So, start by declaring the method in *PlayerNode.h*, like this:

```
#import <SpriteKit/SpriteKit.h>

@interface PlayerNode :┬áSKNode

// returns duration of future movement
- (void)moveToward:(CGPoint)location;

@end
```

Next, switch over to *PlayerNode.m* and add the following implementation:

```
- (void)moveToward:(CGPoint)location {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self removeActionForKey:@"movement"];

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat distance =┬áPointDistance(self.position, location);
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat screenWidth =┬á[UIScreen mainScreen].bounds.size.width;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat duration =┬á2.0 *┬ádistance /┬áscreenWidth;

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self runAction:[SKAction moveTo:location duration:duration]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áwithKey:@"movement"];
}
```

WeÔÇÖll skip the first line for now, returning to it shortly. This method compares the new location to the current position and figures out the distance and the number of pixels to move. Next, it figures out how much time the movement should take, using a numeric constant to set the speed of the overall movement. Finally, it creates an `SKAction` to make the move happen. `SKAction` is a part of Sprite Kit that knows how to make changes to nodes over time, letting you easily animate a nodeÔÇÖs position, size, rotation, transparency, and more. In this case, we are telling the player node to run a simple movement action over a particular duration, and then assigning that action to the key `@"movement."` As you see, this key is the same as the key used in the first line of this method to remove an action. We started off this method by removing any existing action with the same key, so that the user can tap several locations in quick succession without spawning a lot of competing actions trying to move in different ways!

Geometry Calculations

Now youÔÇÖll notice that weÔÇÖve introduced another problem, because Xcode canÔÇÖt find any function called `PointDistance()`. This is one of several simple geometric functions that our app will use to perform calculations using points, vectors, and floats. LetÔÇÖs put this in place now. Use Xcode to make a new file, this time a Header File from the iOS section. Name it *Geometry.h* and give it the following content:

```
#ifndef TextShooter_Geometry_h
#define TextShooter_Geometry_h

// Takes a┬áCGVector and a┬áCGFLoat.
// Returns a┬ánew CGFloat where each component of v┬áhas been multiplied by m.
static inline CGVector VectorMultiply(CGVector v, CGFloat m) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn CGVectorMake(v.dx *┬ám, v.dy *┬ám);
}

// Takes two CGPoints.
// Returns a┬áCGVector representing a┬ádirection from p1 to p2.
static inline CGVector VectorBetweenPoints(CGPoint p1, CGPoint p2) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn CGVectorMake(p2.x -┬áp1.x, p2.y -┬áp1.y);
}

// Takes a┬áCGVector.
// Returns a┬áCGFloat containing the length of the vector, calculated using
// Pythagoras' theorem.
static inline CGFloat VectorLength(CGVector v) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn sqrtf(powf(v.dx, 2) +┬ápowf(v.dy, 2));
}

// Takes two CGPoints. Returns a┬áCGFloat containing the distance between them,
// calculated with Pythagoras' theorem.
static inline CGFloat PointDistance(CGPoint p1, CGPoint p2) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn sqrtf(powf(p2.x -┬áp1.x, 2) +┬ápowf(p2.y -┬áp1.y, 2));
}

#endif
```

These are simple implementations of some common operations that are useful in many games: multiplying vectors, creating vectors pointing from one point to another, and calculating distances. To let the code use these, just add the following import near the top of *PlayerNode.m*:

```
#import "Geometry.h"
```

Now build and run the app. After the playerÔÇÖs ship appears, tap anywhere in the bottom portion of the screen to see that the ship slides left or right to reach the point you tapped. You can tap again before the ship reaches its destination, and it will immediately begin a new animation to move toward the new spot. ThatÔÇÖs fine, but wouldnÔÇÖt it be nice if the playerÔÇÖs ship were a bit livelier in its motion?

Wobbly Bits

LetÔÇÖs give the ship a bit of a wobble as it moves by adding another animation. Add the bold lines to `PlayerNode`ÔÇÖs `moveToward:` method.

```
- (void)moveToward:(CGPoint)location {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self removeActionForKey:@"movement"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self removeActionForKey:@"wobbling"];

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat distance =┬áPointDistance(self.position, location);
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat pixels =┬á[UIScreen mainScreen].bounds.size.width;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat duration =┬á2.0 *┬ádistance /┬ápixels;

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self runAction:[SKAction moveTo:location duration:duration]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áwithKey:@"movement"];

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat wobbleTime =┬á0.3;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat halfWobbleTime =┬áwobbleTime *┬á0.5;
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKAction *wobbling =┬á[SKAction
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ásequence:@[[SKAction scaleXTo:0.2 duration:halfWobbleTime],
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [SKAction scaleXTo:1.0
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áduration:halfWobbleTime]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ]];
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUInteger wobbleCount =┬áduration /┬áwobbleTime;

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self runAction:[SKAction repeatAction:wobbling count:wobbleCount]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áwithKey:@"wobbling"];
}
```

What we just did is similar to the movement action we created earlier, but it differs in some important ways. For the basic movement, we simply calculated the movement duration, and then created and ran a movement action in a single step. This time, itÔÇÖs a little more complicated. First, we define the time for a single ÔÇ£wobbleÔÇØ (the ship may wobble multiple times while moving, but will wobble at a consistent rate throughout). The wobble itself consists of first scaling the ship along the x axis (i.e., its width) to 2/10ths of its normal size, and then scaling it back to it to its full size. Each of these is a single action that is packed together into another kind of action called a **sequence**, which performs all the actions it contains one after another. Next, we figure out how many times this wobble can happen during the duration of the shipÔÇÖs travel and wrap the `wobbling` sequence inside a repeat action, telling it how many complete wobble cycles it should execute. And, as before, we start the method by canceling any previous wobbling action, since we wouldnÔÇÖt want competing wobblers.

Now run the app, and youÔÇÖll see that the ship wobbles pleasantly when moving back and forth. It kind of looks like itÔÇÖs walking!

Creating Your Enemies

So far so good, but this game is going to need some enemies for our players to shoot at. Use Xcode to make a new Cocoa Touch class called `EnemyNode`, using `SKNode` as the parent class. WeÔÇÖre not going to give the enemy class any real behavior just yet, but we will give it an appearance. WeÔÇÖll use the same technique that we used for the player, using text to build the enemyÔÇÖs body. Surely, thereÔÇÖs no text character more intimidating than the letter X, so our enemy will be a letter XÔÇª made of lowercase Xs! Try not to be scared just thinking about that as you add these methods to *EnemyNode.m*:

```
- (instancetype)init {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super init]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.name =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Enemy %p", self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self initNodeGraph];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}

- (void)initNodeGraph {
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *topRow =┬á[SKLabelNode
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier-Bold"];
┬á┬á┬á┬átopRow.fontColor =┬á[SKColor brownColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬átopRow.fontSize =┬á20;
┬á┬á┬á┬átopRow.text =┬á@"x x";
┬á┬á┬á┬átopRow.position =┬áCGPointMake(0, 15);
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:topRow];

┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *middleRow =┬á[SKLabelNode
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álabelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier-Bold"];
┬á┬á┬á┬ámiddleRow.fontColor =┬á[SKColor brownColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬ámiddleRow.fontSize =┬á20;
┬á┬á┬á┬ámiddleRow.text =┬á@"x";
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:middleRow];

┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *bottomRow =┬á[SKLabelNode
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álabelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier-Bold"];
┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomRow.fontColor =┬á[SKColor brownColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomRow.fontSize =┬á20;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomRow.text =┬á@"x x";
┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomRow.position =┬áCGPointMake(0, -15);
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:bottomRow];
}
```

ThereÔÇÖs nothing much new there; weÔÇÖre just adding multiple ÔÇ£rowsÔÇØ of text by shifting the y value for each of their positions.

Putting Enemies in the Scene

Now letÔÇÖs make some enemies appear in the scene by making some changes to *GameScene.m*. First, add the bold lines shown here, near the top:

```
#import "GameScene.h"
#import "PlayerNode.h"
#import "EnemyNode.h"

@interface GameScene ()

@property (strong, nonatomic) PlayerNode *playerNode;
@property (strong, nonatomic) SKNode *enemies;

@end
```

We imported the header for our new enemy class and we added a new property for holding all the enemies that will be added to the level. You might think that weÔÇÖd use an `NSMutableArray` for this, but it turns out that using a plain `SKNode` is perfect for the job. `SKNode` can hold any number of child nodes. And since we need to add all the enemies to the scene anyway, we may as well hold them all in an `SKNode` for easy access.

The next step is to create the `spawnEnemies` method, as shown here:

```
- (void)spawnEnemies {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSUInteger count =┬álog(self.levelNumber) +┬áself.levelNumber;
┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (NSUInteger i┬á= 0; i┬á< count; i++) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áEnemyNode *enemy =┬á[EnemyNode node];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGSize size =┬áself.frame.size;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat x┬á= arc4random_uniform(size.width *┬á0.8)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á+ (size.width *┬á0.1);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat y┬á= arc4random_uniform(size.height *┬á0.5)
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á+ (size.height *┬á0.5);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áenemy.position =┬áCGPointMake(x, y);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.enemies addChild:enemy];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Finally, add these lines near the end of the init`WithSize:levelNumber:` method to create an empty `enemies` node, and then call the `spawnEnemies` method:

```
[self addChild:_playerNode];
_enemies =┬á[SKNode node];
[self addChild:_enemies];
[self spawnEnemies];
```

Since we added the `enemies` node to the scene, any child enemy nodes we add to the `enemies` node will also appear in the scene.

Now run the app, and youÔÇÖll see a dreadful enemy placed randomly in the upper portion of the screen (see [Figure 17-7](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig7)). DonÔÇÖt you wish you could shoot it?

![9781484202005_Fig17-07.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-07.jpg)

[Figure 17-7](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig7). IÔÇÖm sure youÔÇÖll agree that the X made of Xs just needs to be shot

Start Shooting

ItÔÇÖs time to implement the next logical step in the development of this game: letting the player attack the enemies. We want the player to be able to tap anywhere in the upper 80% of the screen to shoot a bullet at the enemies. WeÔÇÖre going to use the **physics engine** included in Sprite Kit both to move our playerÔÇÖs bullets and to let us know when a bullet collides with an enemy.

But first, what is this thing we call a physics engine? Basically, a physics engine is a software component that keeps track of multiple physical objects (commonly referred to as **bodies**) in a world, along with the forces that are acting upon them. It also makes sure that everything moves in a realistic way. It can take into account the force of gravity, handle collisions between objects (so that objects donÔÇÖt occupy the same space simultaneously), and even simulate physical characteristics like friction and bounciness.

ItÔÇÖs important to understand that a physics engine is typically separate from a graphics engine. Apple provides convenient APIs to let us work with both, but they are essentially separate. ItÔÇÖs common to have objects in your display, such as our labels that show the current level number and remaining lives, that are completely separate from the physics engine. And itÔÇÖs possible to create objects that have a physics body, but donÔÇÖt actually display anything at all.

Defining Your Physics Categories

One of the things that the Sprite Kit physics engine lets us do is to assign objects to several distinct **physics categories**. A physics category has nothing to do with Objective-C categories. Instead, a physics category is a way to group related objects so that the physics engine can handle collisions between them in different ways. In this game, for example, weÔÇÖll create three categories: one for enemies, one for the player, and one for player missiles. We definitely want the physics engine to concern itself with collisions between enemies and player missiles, but we probably want it to ignore collisions between player missiles and the player itself. This is easy to set up using physics categories.

So, letÔÇÖs create the categories weÔÇÖre going to need. Press ![image](images/flower.jpg)**N** to bring up the new file assistant, choose **Header File** from the iOS section, and press **Next**. Give the new header file the name *PhysicsCategories.h* and save it, and then add the following code to it:

```
#ifndef TextShooter_PhysicsCategories_h
#define TextShooter_PhysicsCategories_h

typedef NS_OPTIONS(uint32_t, PhysicsCategory) {
┬á┬á┬á┬áPlayerCategory┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á=┬á┬á1 << 1,
┬á┬á┬á┬áEnemyCategory┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á =┬á┬á1 << 2,
┬á┬á┬á┬áPlayerMissileCategory =┬á┬á1 << 3
};

#endif
```

Here we declared three category constants. Note that the categories work as a bitmask, so each of them must be a power of two. We can easily do this by bit-shifting. These are set up as a bitmask in order to simplify the physics engineÔÇÖs API a little bit. With bitmasks, we can logically *OR* several values together. This enables us to use a single API call to tell the physics engine how to deal with collisions between many different layers. WeÔÇÖll see this in action soon.

Creating the BulletNode Class

Now that weÔÇÖve laid some groundwork, letÔÇÖs create some bullets so we can start shooting.

Create a new Cocoa Touch class called `BulletNode`, once again using `SKNode` as its superclass. Start in the header file, where youÔÇÖll declare the two public methods this class will have:

```
#import <SpriteKit/SpriteKit.h>

@interface BulletNode :┬áSKNode

+ (instancetype)bulletFrom:(CGPoint)start toward:(CGPoint)destination;
- (void)applyRecurringForce;

@end
```

The first method is a factory method for creating new instances of the class. The second is one that youÔÇÖll need to call from your scene each frame, to tell the bullet to move. Now switch over to *BulletNode.m* to start implementing this class.

The first thing weÔÇÖre going to do is import a header for our special geometry functions and physics categories. The second step is to add a class extension with a single property, which will contain this bulletÔÇÖs thrust vector:

```
#import "BulletNode.h"
#import "PhysicsCategories.h"
#import "Geometry.h"

@interface BulletNode ()

@property (assign, nonatomic) CGVector thrust;

@end
```

Next, we implement an `init` method. Like other `init` methods in this application, this is where we create the object graph for our bullet. This will consist of a single dot. While weÔÇÖre at it, letÔÇÖs also configure physics for this class by creating and configuring an `SKPhysicsBody` instance and attaching it to `self`. In the process, we tell the new body what category it belongs to and which categories should be checked for collisions with this object.

```
@implementation BulletNode

- (instancetype)init {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super init]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *dot =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádot.fontColor =┬á[SKColor blackColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádot.fontSize =┬á40;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádot.text =┬á@".";
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:dot];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKPhysicsBody *body =┬á[SKPhysicsBody bodyWithCircleOfRadius:1];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.dynamic =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.categoryBitMask =┬áPlayerMissileCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.contactTestBitMask =┬áEnemyCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.collisionBitMask =┬áEnemyCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.mass =┬á0.01;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.physicsBody =┬ábody;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.name =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Bullet %p", self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

Applying Physics

Next, weÔÇÖll create the factory method that creates a new bullet and gives it a thrust vector that the physics engine will use to propel the bullet toward its target:

```
+ (instancetype)bulletFrom:(CGPoint)start toward:(CGPoint)destination {
┬á┬á┬á┬áBulletNode *bullet =┬á[[self alloc] init];

┬á┬á┬á┬ábullet.position =┬ástart;

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGVector movement =┬áVectorBetweenPoints(start, destination);
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat magnitude =┬áVectorLength(movement);
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (magnitude == 0.0f) return nil;

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGVector scaledMovement =┬áVectorMultiply(movement, 1┬á/ magnitude);

┬á┬á┬á┬áCGFloat thrustMagnitude =┬á100.0;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábullet.thrust =┬áVectorMultiply(scaledMovement, thrustMagnitude);

┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn bullet;
}
```

The basic calculations are pretty simple. We first determine a `movement` vector that points from the start location to the destination, and then we determine its `magnitude` (length). Dividing the `movement` vector by its `magnitude` produces a normalized **unit vector**, a vector that points in the same direction as the original, but is exactly one unit long (a unit, in this case, is the same as a ÔÇ£pointÔÇØ on the screenÔÇöe.g., two pixels on a Retina device, one pixel on older devices). Creating a unit vector is very useful because we can multiply that by a fixed magnitude (in this case, 100) to determine a uniformly powerful thrust vector, no matter how far away the user tapped the screen.

The final piece of code we need to add to this class is this method, which applies thrust to the physics body. WeÔÇÖll call this once per frame, from inside the scene:

```
- (void)applyRecurringForce {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.physicsBody applyForce:self.thrust];
}
```

Adding Bullets to the Scene

Now switch over to *GameScene.m* to add bullets to the scene itself. For starters, import the header for the new class near the top. Next, add another property to contain all bullets in a single `SKNode`, just as you did earlier for enemies:

```
#import "GameScene.h"
#import "PlayerNode.h"
#import "EnemyNode.h"
#import "BulletNode.h"

@interface GameScene ()

@property (strong, nonatomic) PlayerNode *playerNode;
@property (strong, nonatomic) SKNode *enemies;
@property (strong, nonatomic) SKNode *playerBullets;

@end
```

Find the section of the `initWithSize:levelNumber:` method where you previously added the enemies. ThatÔÇÖs the place to set up the `playerBullets` node, too.

```
[self spawnEnemies];
_playerBullets =┬á[SKNode node];
[self addChild:_playerBullets];
```

Now weÔÇÖre ready to code the actual missile launches. Add this `else` clause to the `touchesBegan:withEvent:` method, so that all taps in the upper part of the screen shoot a bullet instead of moving the ship:

```
- (void)touchesBegan:(NSSet *)touches withEvent:(UIEvent *)event {
┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (UITouch *touch in touches) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint location =┬á[touch locationInNode:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (location.y < CGRectGetHeight(self.frame) *┬á0.2 )┬á{
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint target =┬áCGPointMake(location.x,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á self.playerNode.position.y);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.playerNode moveToward:target];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áBulletNode *bullet =┬á[BulletNode
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á bulletFrom:self.playerNode.position
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á toward:location];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.playerBullets addChild:bullet];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

That adds the bullet, but none of the bullets we add will actually move unless we tell them to by applying thrust every frame. Our scene already contains an empty method called `update:`. This method is called each frame, and thatÔÇÖs the perfect place to do any game logic that needs to occur in each frame. Rather than updating all our bullets right in that method, however, we put that code in a separate method that we call from the `update:` method:

```
- (void)update:(CFTimeInterval)currentTime {
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateBullets];
}

- (void)updateBullets {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSMutableArray *bulletsToRemove =┬á[NSMutableArray array];
┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (BulletNode *bullet in self.playerBullets.children) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Remove any bullets that have moved off-screen
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!CGRectContainsPoint(self.frame, bullet.position)) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// mark bullet for removal
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[bulletsToRemove addObject:bullet];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontinue;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Apply thrust to remaining bullets
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[bullet applyRecurringForce];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.playerBullets removeChildrenInArray:bulletsToRemove];
}
```

Before telling each bullet to apply its recurring force, we also check whether each bullet is still on-screen. Any bullet thatÔÇÖs gone off-screen is put into a temporary array; and then, at the end, those are swept out of the `playerBullets` node. Note that this two-stage process is necessary because the `for` loop at work in this method is iterating over all children in the `playerBullets` node. Making changes to a collection while youÔÇÖre iterating over it is never a good idea, and it can easily lead to a crash.

Now build and run the app, and youÔÇÖll see that, in addition to moving the playerÔÇÖs ship, you can make it shoot missiles upward by tapping on the screen (see [Figure 17-8](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig8)). Neat!

![9781484202005_Fig17-08.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-08.jpg)

[Figure 17-8](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig8). Shooting up a storm!

Attacking Enemies with Physics

A couple of important gameplay elements are still missing from our game. The enemies never attack us, and we canÔÇÖt yet get rid of the enemies by shooting them. LetÔÇÖs take care of the latter right now. WeÔÇÖre going to set things up so that shooting an enemy has the effect of dislodging it from the spot where itÔÇÖs currently fixed on the screen. This feature will use the physics engine for all the heavy lifting, and it will involve making changes to `PlayerNode`, `EnemyNode`, and `GameScene`.

For starters, letÔÇÖs add physics bodies to our nodes that donÔÇÖt already have them. Start with *EnemyNode.m*, adding this `#import` statement near the top:

```
#import "PhysicsCategories.h"
```

Next, add the following line to the `init` method:

```
- (instancetype)init {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super init]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.name =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Enemy %p", self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self initNodeGraph];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self initPhysicsBody];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

Now add the code to really set up the physics body. This is pretty similar to what you did earlier for the `PlayerBullet` class:

```
- (void)initPhysicsBody {
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKPhysicsBody *body =┬á[SKPhysicsBody bodyWithRectangleOfSize:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á CGSizeMake(40, 40)];
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.affectedByGravity =┬áNO;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.categoryBitMask =┬áEnemyCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.contactTestBitMask =┬áPlayerCategory|EnemyCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.mass =┬á0.2;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.angularDamping =┬á0.0f;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.linearDamping =┬á0.0f;
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.physicsBody =┬ábody;
}
```

Then select *PlayerNode.m*, where youÔÇÖre going to do a pretty similar set of things. First, add the following `#import` near the top:

```
#import "PhysicsCategories.h"
```

Follow up by adding the bold line shown here to the `init` method:

```
- (instancetype)init {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super init]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.name =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Player %p", self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self initNodeGraph];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self initPhysicsBody];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

Finally, add the new `initPhysicsBody` method:

```
- (void)initPhysicsBody {
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKPhysicsBody *body =┬á[SKPhysicsBody bodyWithRectangleOfSize:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á CGSizeMake(20, 20)];
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.affectedByGravity =┬áNO;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.categoryBitMask =┬áPlayerCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.contactTestBitMask =┬áEnemyCategory;
┬á┬á┬á┬ábody.collisionBitMask =┬á0;

┬á┬á┬á┬áself.physicsBody =┬ábody;
}
```

At this point, you can run the app and see that your bullets now have the ability to knock enemies into space. However, youÔÇÖll also see thereÔÇÖs a problem here. When you start the game and then send the lone enemy hurtling into space, youÔÇÖre stuck! This is probably a good time to add level management to the game.

Finishing Levels

We need to enhance `GameScene` so that it knows when itÔÇÖs time to move to the next level. It can figure this out simply enough by looking at the number of available enemies. If it finds that there arenÔÇÖt any on-screen, then the level is over, and the game should transition to the next.

Keeping Tabs on the Enemies

Begin by adding this `updateEnemies` method. It works a lot like the `updateBullets` method added earlier:

```
- (void)updateEnemies {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSMutableArray *enemiesToRemove =┬á[NSMutableArray array];
┬á┬á┬á┬áfor (SKNode *node in self.enemies.children) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Remove any enemies that have moved off-screen
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (!CGRectContainsPoint(self.frame, node.position)) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// mark enemy for removal
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[enemiesToRemove addObject:node];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ácontinue;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([enemiesToRemove count] > 0) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.enemies removeChildrenInArray:enemiesToRemove];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

That takes care of removing each enemy from the levelÔÇÖs `enemies` array each time one goes off-screen. Now letÔÇÖs modify the `update:` method, telling it to call `updateEnemies`, as well as a new method we havenÔÇÖt yet implemented:

```
- (void)update:(CFTimeInterval)currentTime {
┬á┬á┬á┬á/* Called before each frame is rendered */
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.finished) return;

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateBullets];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateEnemies];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self checkForNextLevel];
}
```

We started out that method by checking the `finished` property. Since weÔÇÖre about to add code that can officially end a level, we want to be sure that we donÔÇÖt keep doing additional processing after the level is complete! Then, just as weÔÇÖre checking each frame to see if any bullets or enemies have gone off-screen, weÔÇÖre going to call `checkForNextLevel` each frame to see if the current level is complete. LetÔÇÖs add this method:

```
- (void)checkForNextLevel {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([self.enemies.children count] == 0) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self goToNextLevel];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Transitioning to the Next Levels

The `checkForNextLevel` method in turn calls another method we havenÔÇÖt yet implemented. The `goToNextLevel` method marks this level as finished, displays some text on the screen to let the player know, and then starts the next level:

```
- (void)goToNextLevel {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.finished =┬áYES;

┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *label =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.text =┬á@"Level Complete!";
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.fontColor =┬á[SKColor blueColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.fontSize =┬á32;
┬á┬á┬á┬álabel.position =┬áCGPointMake(self.frame.size.width *┬á0.5,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á self.frame.size.height *┬á0.5);
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:label];

┬á┬á┬á┬áGameScene *nextLevel =┬á[[GameScene alloc]
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áinitWithSize:self.frame.size
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬álevelNumber:self.levelNumber +┬á1];
┬á┬á┬á┬ánextLevel.playerLives =┬áself.playerLives;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.view presentScene:nextLevel
┬á┬á transition:[SKTransition flipHorizontalWithDuration:1.0]];
}
```

The second half of the `goToNextLevel` method creates a new instance of `GameScene` and gives it all the start values it needs. It then tells the view to present the new scene, using a transition to smooth things over. The `SKTransition` class lets us pick from a variety of transition styles. Run the app and complete a level to see what this one looks like (see [Figure 17-9](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig9)).

![9781484202005_Fig17-09.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-09.jpg)

[Figure 17-9](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig9). Here you see a snapshot taken during the end-of-level screen-flipping transition

The transition in use here makes it looks like weÔÇÖre flipping a card over its horizontal axis, but there are plenty more to choose from! See the documentation or header file for `SKTransition` to see more possibilities. WeÔÇÖll use a couple more variations later in this chapter.

Customizing Collisions

Now weÔÇÖve got a game that you can really play. You can clear level after level by knocking enemies upward off the screen. ThatÔÇÖs OK, but thereÔÇÖs really not much challenge! We mentioned earlier that having enemies attack the player is one piece of missing gameplay, and now itÔÇÖs time to make that happen. WeÔÇÖre going to make things a little harder by making the enemies fall down when theyÔÇÖre bumped, either from being hit by a bullet or from being touched by another enemy. We also want to make it so that being hit by a falling enemy takes a life away from the player. You also may have noticed that after a bullet hits an enemy, the bullet squiggles its way around the enemy and continues on its upward trajectory, which is pretty weird. WeÔÇÖre going to tackle all these things by implementing a collision-handling routine in *GameScene.m*.

The method for handling detected collisions is a delegate method for the `SKPhysicsWorld` class. Our scene has a physics world by default, but we need to set it up a little bit before it will tell us anything. For starters, itÔÇÖs good to let the compiler know that weÔÇÖre going to implement a delegate protocol, so letÔÇÖs add this declaration to the class extension declaration near the top of the *GameScene.m* file:

```
@interface GameScene () <SKPhysicsContactDelegate>
```

We still need to configure the world a bit (giving it a slightly less cruel amount of gravity) and tell it who its delegate is. To do so, we add these bold lines near the end of the `initWithSize:levelNumber:` method:

```
self.physicsWorld.gravity =┬áCGVectorMake(0, -1);
self.physicsWorld.contactDelegate =┬áself;
```

Now that weÔÇÖve set the physics worldÔÇÖs `contactDelegate` to be the `GameScene`, we can implement the relevant delegate method. The core of the method looks like this:

```
- (void)didBeginContact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (contact.bodyA.categoryBitMask == contact.bodyB.categoryBitMask) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Both bodies are in the same category
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *nodeA =┬ácontact.bodyA.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *nodeB =┬ácontact.bodyB.node;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// What do we do with these nodes?
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *attacker =┬ánil;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *attackee =┬ánil;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (contact.bodyA.categoryBitMask > contact.bodyB.categoryBitMask) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Body A┬áis attacking Body B
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattacker =┬ácontact.bodyA.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattackee =┬ácontact.bodyB.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Body B┬áis attacking Body A
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattacker =┬ácontact.bodyB.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattackee =┬ácontact.bodyA.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([attackee isKindOfClass:[PlayerNode class]]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.playerLives--;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// What do we do with the attacker and the attackee?
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Go ahead and add that method, but if you look at it right now, youÔÇÖll see that it doesnÔÇÖt really do much yet. In fact, the only concrete result of that method is to reduce the number of player lives each time a falling enemy hits the playerÔÇÖs ship. But the enemies arenÔÇÖt falling yet!

The idea behind this implementation is to look at the two colliding objects and to figure out whether they are of the same category (in which case, they are ÔÇ£friendsÔÇØ to one another) or if they are of different categories. If they are of different categories, we have to determine who is attacking whom. If you look at the order of the categories declared in *PhysicsCategories.h*, youÔÇÖll see that they are specified in order of increased ÔÇ£attackynessÔÇØ: `Player` nodes can be attacked by `Enemy` nodes, which in turn can be attacked by `PlayerMissile` nodes. That means that we can use a simple greater-than comparison to figure out who is the ÔÇ£attackerÔÇØ in this scenario.

For the sake of simplicity and modularity, we donÔÇÖt really want the scene to decide how each object should react to being attacked by an enemy or bumped by another object. ItÔÇÖs much better to build those details into the affected node classes themselves. But, as you see in the method weÔÇÖve got, the only thing weÔÇÖre sure of is that each side has an `SKNode` instance. Rather than coding up a big chain of `if`-`else` statements to ask each node which `SKNode` subclass it belongs to, we can use regular polymorphism to let each of our node classes handle things in its own way. In order for that to work, we have to add methods to `SKNode`, with default implementations that do nothing, and let our subclasses override them where appropriate. This calls for a category! Not a Sprite Kit physics category this time, but a genuine Objective-C `@category` definition.

Adding a Category to SKNode

To add a category to `SKNode`, right-click the *TextShooter* folder in XcodeÔÇÖs Project Navigator and choose **New FileÔÇª** from the pop-up menu. From the assistantÔÇÖs iOS/Source section, choose **Objective-C File**, and then click **Next**. Give it a File name of *Extra*, select **Category** as the File Type, and choose `SKNode` as the class to which the category is being added. Now click **Next** again and create the files. Select the category header file *SKNode+Extra.h* and add the bold method declarations shown here:

```
#import <SpriteKit/SpriteKit.h>

@interface SKNode (Extra)

- (void)receiveAttacker:(SKNode *)attacker contact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact;
- (void)friendlyBumpFrom:(SKNode *)node;

@end
```

Switch over to the matching *.m* file and enter the following empty definitions:

```
#import "SKNode+Extra.h"

@implementation SKNode (Extra)

- (void)receiveAttacker:(SKNode *)attacker contact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// default implementation does nothing
}

- (void)friendlyBumpFrom:(SKNode *)node {
┬á┬á┬á┬á// default implementation does nothing
}

@end
```

Now head back over to *GameScene.m* to finish up its part of the collision handling. Start by adding a new header at the top:

```
#import "GameScene.h"
#import "PlayerNode.h"
#import "EnemyNode.h"
#import "BulletNode.h"
#import "SKNode+Extra.h"
```

Next, go back to the `didBeginContact:` method, where youÔÇÖll add the bits that actually do some work:

```
- (void)didBeginContact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (contact.bodyA.categoryBitMask == contact.bodyB.categoryBitMask) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Both bodies are in the same category
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *nodeA =┬ácontact.bodyA.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *nodeB =┬ácontact.bodyB.node;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// What do we do with these nodes?
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[nodeA friendlyBumpFrom:nodeB];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[nodeB friendlyBumpFrom:nodeA];
┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *attacker =┬ánil;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKNode *attackee =┬ánil;

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif (contact.bodyA.categoryBitMask > contact.bodyB.categoryBitMask) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Body A┬áis attacking Body B
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattacker =┬ácontact.bodyA.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattackee =┬ácontact.bodyB.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á} else {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// Body B┬áis attacking Body A
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattacker =┬ácontact.bodyB.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áattackee =┬ácontact.bodyA.node;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áif ([attackee isKindOfClass:[PlayerNode class]]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.playerLives--;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á// What do we do with the attacker and the attackee?
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[attackee receiveAttacker:attacker contact:contact];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.playerBullets removeChildrenInArray:@[attacker]];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.enemies removeChildrenInArray:@[attacker]];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

All we added here were a few calls to our new methods. If the collision is ÔÇ£friendly fire,ÔÇØ such as two enemies bumping into each other, weÔÇÖll tell each of them that it received a friendly bump from the other. Otherwise, after figuring out who attacked whom, we tell the attackee that itÔÇÖs come under attack from another object. Finally, we remove the attacker from whichever of the `playerBullets` or `enemies` nodes it may be in. We tell each of those nodes to remove the attacker, even though it can only be in one of them, but thatÔÇÖs OK. Telling a node to remove a child it doesnÔÇÖt have isnÔÇÖt an errorÔÇöit just has no effect.

Adding Custom Collision Behavior to Enemies

Now that all thatÔÇÖs in place, we can implement some specific behaviors for our nodes by overriding the category methods we added to `SKNode`.

Select *EnemyNode.m*. At the top of the file, add an import of *Geometry.h*:

```
#import "PhysicsCategories.h"
#import "Geometry.h"

@implementation EnemyNode
```

Next, add the following two methods:

```
- (void)friendlyBumpFrom:(SKNode *)node {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.physicsBody.affectedByGravity =┬áYES;
}

- (void)receiveAttacker:(SKNode *)attacker contact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.physicsBody.affectedByGravity =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGVector force =┬áVectorMultiply(attacker.physicsBody.velocity,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á contact.collisionImpulse);
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint myContact =┬á[self.scene convertPoint:contact.contactPoint
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átoNode:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.physicsBody applyForce:force
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á atPoint:myContact];
}
```

The first of those, `friendlyBumpFrom``:`, simply turns on gravity for the affected enemy. So, if one enemy is in motion and bumps into another, the second enemy will suddenly notice gravity and start falling downward.

The `receiveAttacker:contact:` method, which is called if the enemy is hit by a bullet, first turns on gravity for the enemy. However, it also uses the contact data that was passed in to figure out just where the contact occurred and applies a force to that point, giving it an extra push in the direction that the bullet was fired.

Showing Accurate Player Lives

Run the game, and youÔÇÖll see that you can shoot at enemies to knock them down. YouÔÇÖll also see that any other enemies bumped into by a falling enemy will fall, as well.

**Note**┬á┬áAt the start of each level, the world performs one step of its physics simulation to make sure that there arenÔÇÖt physics bodies overlapping each other. This will produce an interesting side effect at higher levels, since there will be an increasing chance that multiple randomly placed enemies will occupy overlapping spaces. Whenever that happens, the enemies will be immediately shifted so they no longer overlap, and our collision-handling code will be triggered, which subsequently turns on gravity and lets them fall! This behavior wasnÔÇÖt anything we planned on when we started building this game, but it turns out to be a happy accident that makes higher levels progressively more difficult, so weÔÇÖre letting physics run its course!

If you let enemies hit you as they fall, the number of player lives decreases, butÔÇª hey wait, it just shows *5* all the time! The Lives display is set up when the level is created, but itÔÇÖs never updated after that. Fortunately, this is easily fixed by implementing the `setPlayerLives:` setter in *GameScene.m* instead of using the automatically synthesized setter, like this:

```
- (void)setPlayerLives:(NSUInteger)playerLives┬á┬á{
┬á┬á┬á┬á_playerLives =┬áplayerLives;
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *lives =┬á(id)[self childNodeWithName:@"LivesLabel"];
┬á┬á┬á┬álives.text =┬á[NSString stringWithFormat:@"Lives: %lu",
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á(unsigned long)_playerLives];
}
```

The preceding snippet uses the name we previously associated with the label (in the `initWithSize:level:` method) to find the label again and set a new text value. Play the game again, and youÔÇÖll see that, as you let enemies rain down on your player, the number of lives will decrease to zero. And then the game doesnÔÇÖt end. After the next hit, you end up with a very large number of lives indeed, as you can see in [Figure 17-10](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig10).

![9781484202005_Fig17-10.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-10.jpg)

[Figure 17-10](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig10). ThatÔÇÖs a lot of lives

So whatÔÇÖs going on here? Well, we are using an unsigned integer to hold the number of lives. And when youÔÇÖre using unsigned integers and dip below zero, you sort of wrap around that zero boundary and end up with the maximum allowed unsigned integer value instead!

The reason this problem appears is really because we havenÔÇÖt written any code to detect the end of the game; that is, the point in time when the number of player lives hits zero. WeÔÇÖll do that soon, but first letÔÇÖs make our on-screen collisions a bit more stimulating.

Spicing Things Up with Particles

One of the nice features of Sprite Kit is the inclusion of a particle system. Particle systems are used in games to create visual effects simulating smoke, fire, explosions, and more. Right now, whenever our bullets hit an enemy or an enemy hits the player, the attacking object simply blinks out of existence. LetÔÇÖs make a couple of particle systems to improve this situation!

Start out by pressing ![image](images/flower.jpg)**N** to bring up the new file assistant. Select the **iOS/Resource** section on the left, and then choose **SpriteKit Particle File** on the right. Click **Next**, and on the following screen choose the **Spark** particle template. Click **Next** again and name this file *MissileExplosion.sks*.

Your First Particle

YouÔÇÖll see that Xcode creates the particle file and also adds a new resource called *spark.png* to the project. At the same time, the entire Xcode editing area switches over to the new particle file, showing you a huge, animated exploding thing.

We donÔÇÖt want something quite this extravagant and enormous when our bullets hit enemies, so letÔÇÖs reconfigure this thing. All the properties that define this particleÔÇÖs animation are available in the SKNode Inspector, which you can bring up by pressing **Opt-Cmd-7**. [Figure 17-11](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig11) shows both the massive explosion and the inspector.

![9781484202005_Fig17-11.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-11.jpg)

[Figure 17-11](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig11). Explosion city! The parameters shown on the right define how the default particle looks

Now, for our bullet hit, letÔÇÖs make it a much smaller explosion. It will have a whole different set of parameters, all of which you configure right in the inspector. First, fix the colors to match what our game looks like by clicking the small color well in the Color Ramp at the bottom and setting it to black. Next, change the Background color to white and change the Blend Mode to *Alpha*. Now youÔÇÖll see that the flaming fountain has turned all inky.

The rest of the parameters are all numeric. Change them one at a time, setting them all as shown in [Figure 17-12](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig12). At each step of the way, youÔÇÖll see the particle effect change until it eventually reaches its target appearance.

![9781484202005_Fig17-12.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-12.jpg)

[Figure 17-12](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig12). This is the final missile explosion particle effect we want

Now make another particle system, once again using the Spark template. Name this one *EnemyExplosion.sks* and set its parameters as shown in [Figure 17-13](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig13).

![9781484202005_Fig17-13.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-13.jpg)

[Figure 17-13](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig13). HereÔÇÖs the enemy explosion we want to create. In case youÔÇÖre seeing this book in black and white, the color weÔÇÖve chosen in the Color Ramp at the bottom is deep red

**Note**┬á┬áAfter adding the second particle file, you may find two copies of the file spark.png in the Project Navigator and a warning in the Activity View. Fix this by right-clicking on one of the files and selecing Delete.

Putting Particles into the Scene

Now letÔÇÖs start putting these particles to use. Switch over to *EnemyNode.m* and add the bold code shown here to the bottom of the `receiveAttacker:contact:` method:

```
- (void)receiveAttacker:(SKNode *)attacker contact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.physicsBody.affectedByGravity =┬áYES;
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGVector force =┬áVectorMultiply(attacker.physicsBody.velocity,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á contact.collisionImpulse);
┬á┬á┬á┬áCGPoint myContact =┬á[self.scene convertPoint:contact.contactPoint
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átoNode:self];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.physicsBody applyForce:force
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á atPoint:myContact];

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *path =┬á[[NSBundle mainBundle] pathForResource:@"MissileExplosion"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ofType:@"sks"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKEmitterNode *explosion =┬á[NSKeyedUnarchiver unarchiveObjectWithFile:path];
┬á┬á┬á┬áexplosion.numParticlesToEmit =┬á20;
┬á┬á┬á┬áexplosion.position =┬ácontact.contactPoint;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.scene addChild:explosion];
}
```

Run the game, shoot some enemies, and youÔÇÖll see a nice little explosion where each bullet hits an enemy, as shown in [Figure 17-14](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig14).

![9781484202005_Fig17-14.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-14.jpg)

[Figure 17-14](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig14). Bullets smash nicely after impact

Nice! Now letÔÇÖs do something similar for those times an enemy smashes into a playerÔÇÖs ship. Select *PlayerNode.m* and add this method:

```
- (void)receiveAttacker:(SKNode *)attacker contact:(SKPhysicsContact *)contact {
┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *path =┬á[[NSBundle mainBundle] pathForResource:@"EnemyExplosion"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ofType:@"sks"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKEmitterNode *explosion =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [NSKeyedUnarchiver unarchiveObjectWithFile:path];
┬á┬á┬á┬áexplosion.numParticlesToEmit =┬á50;
┬á┬á┬á┬áexplosion.position =┬ácontact.contactPoint;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.scene addChild:explosion];
}
```

Play again, and youÔÇÖll see a nice red splat every time an enemy hits the player, as shown in [Figure 17-15](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig15).

![9781484202005_Fig17-15.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-15.jpg)

[Figure 17-15](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig15). Ouch!

These changes are pretty simple, but they improve the feel of the game substantially. Now when things collide, you have visual c.uences and can see that something happened.

The End Game

As we mentioned before, we currently have a small problem in the game. When the number of lives hits zero, we need to end the game. What weÔÇÖll do is create a new scene class to transition to when the game is over. YouÔÇÖve seen us do a scene transition before, when moving from one level to the next. This will be similar, but with a new class.

So, create a new iOS/Cocoa Touch class. Use `SKScene` as the parent class and name the new class `GameOverScene`.

WeÔÇÖll start with a very simple implementation that just displays ÔÇ£Game OverÔÇØ text and does nothing more. WeÔÇÖll accomplish this by adding this code to the `@implementation` in *GameOverScene.m*:

```
- (instancetype)initWithSize:(CGSize)size {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super initWithSize:size]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.backgroundColor =┬á[SKColor purpleColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *text =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átext.text =┬á@"Game Over";
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átext.fontColor =┬á[SKColor whiteColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átext.fontSize =┬á50;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átext.position =┬áCGPointMake(self.frame.size.width *┬á0.5,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.frame.size.height *┬á0.5);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:text];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}
```

Now letÔÇÖs switch back to *GameScene.m*. WeÔÇÖll need to import the header for the new scene at the top:

```
#import "GameScene.h"
#import "PlayerNode.h"
#import "EnemyNode.h"
#import "BulletNode.h"
#import "SKNode+Extra.h"
#import "GameOverScene.h"
```

The basic action of what to do when the game ends is defined by a new method called `triggerGameOver`. Here, we show both an extra explosion and kick off a transition to the new scene we just created:

```
- (void)triggerGameOver {
┬á┬á┬á┬áself.finished =┬áYES;

┬á┬á┬á┬áNSString *path =┬á[[NSBundle mainBundle] pathForResource:@"EnemyExplosion"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á ofType:@"sks"];
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKEmitterNode *explosion =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [NSKeyedUnarchiver unarchiveObjectWithFile:path];
┬á┬á┬á┬áexplosion.numParticlesToEmit =┬á200;
┬á┬á┬á┬áexplosion.position =┬á_playerNode.position;
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:explosion];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[_playerNode removeFromParent];

┬á┬á┬á┬áSKTransition *transition =
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á [SKTransition doorsOpenVerticalWithDuration:1.0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKScene *gameOver =┬á[[GameOverScene alloc] initWithSize:self.frame.size];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.view presentScene:gameOver transition:transition];
}
```

Next, create this new method that will check for the end of the game, call `triggerGameOver` if itÔÇÖs time, and return either `YES` to indicate the game ended or `NO` to indicate that itÔÇÖs still on:

```
- (BOOL)checkForGameOver {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.playerLives == 0) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self triggerGameOver];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn YES;
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn NO;
}
```

Finally, add a check to the existing `update:` method. It checks for the game-over state and only checks for a potential next-level transition if the game is still going. Otherwise, thereÔÇÖs a risk that the final enemy on a level could take the playerÔÇÖs final life and trigger two scene transitions at once!

```
- (void)update:(CFTimeInterval)currentTime {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self.finished) return;

┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateBullets];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self updateEnemies];
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (![self checkForGameOver]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self checkForNextLevel];
┬á┬á┬á┬á}
}
```

Now run the game again, let falling enemies damage your ship five times, and youÔÇÖll see the Game Over screen, as shown in [Figure 17-16](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig16).

![9781484202005_Fig17-16.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-16.jpg)

[Figure 17-16](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig16). ThatÔÇÖs it, man. Game over, manÔÇögame over

At Last, a Beginning: Create a StartScene

This leads us to another problem: What do we do after the game is over? We could allow the player to tap to restart the game; but while thinking of that, a thought crossed my mind. ShouldnÔÇÖt this game have some sort of start screen, so the player isnÔÇÖt immediately thrust into a game at launch time? And shouldnÔÇÖt the game-over screen lead you back there? Of course, the answer to both questions is yes! Go ahead and create another new iOS/Cocoa Touch class, once again using `SKScene` as the superclass, and this time naming it `StartScene`.

WeÔÇÖre going to make a super-simple start scene here. All it will do is display some text and start the game when the user taps anywhere. Add all the bold code shown here to *StartScene.m* to complete this class:

```
#import "StartScene.h"
#import "GameScene.h"

@implementation StartScene

- (instancetype)initWithSize:(CGSize)size {
┬á┬á┬á┬áif (self =┬á[super initWithSize:size]) {
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.backgroundColor =┬á[SKColor greenColor];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *topLabel =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átopLabel.text =┬á@"TextShooter";
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átopLabel.fontColor =┬á[SKColor blackColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átopLabel.fontSize =┬á48;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬átopLabel.position =┬áCGPointMake(self.frame.size.width *┬á0.5,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.frame.size.height *┬á0.7);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:topLabel];

┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKLabelNode *bottomLabel =┬á[SKLabelNode labelNodeWithFontNamed:
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á@"Courier"];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomLabel.text =┬á@"Touch anywhere to start";
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomLabel.fontColor =┬á[SKColor blackColor];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomLabel.fontSize =┬á20;
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ábottomLabel.position =┬áCGPointMake(self.frame.size.width *┬á0.5,
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áself.frame.size.height *┬á0.3);
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self addChild:bottomLabel];

┬á┬á┬á┬á}
┬á┬á┬á┬áreturn self;
}

- (void)touchesBegan:(NSSet *)touches withEvent:(UIEvent *)event {
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKTransition *transition =┬á[SKTransition doorwayWithDuration:1.0];
┬á┬á┬á┬áSKScene *game =┬á[[GameScene alloc] initWithSize:self.frame.size];
┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.view presentScene:game transition:transition];
}

@end
```

Now go back to *GameOverScene.m*, so we can make the game-over scene perform a transition to the start scene. Add this header import:

```
#import "GameOverScene.h"
#import "StartScene.h"
```

And then add the following code:

```
- (void)didMoveToView:(SKView *)view {
┬á┬á┬á┬ádispatch_after(
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádispatch_time(DISPATCH_TIME_NOW, (int64_t)(3.0 *┬áNSEC_PER_SEC)),
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬ádispatch_get_main_queue(), ^{
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKTransition *transition =┬á[SKTransition flipVerticalWithDuration:1.0];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áSKScene *start =┬á[[StartScene alloc] initWithSize:self.frame.size];
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á[self.view presentScene:start transition:transition];
┬á┬á┬á┬á});
}
```

As you saw earlier, the `didMoveToView:` method is called on any scene after itÔÇÖs been put in place in a view. Here, we simply trigger a three-second pause, followed by a transition back to the start scene.

ThereÔÇÖs just one more piece of the puzzle to make all our scenes transition to each other as they should. We need to change the app startup procedure so that, instead of jumping right into the game, it shows us the start screen instead. This takes us back to *GameViewController.m*, where we first import the header for our start scene:

```
#import "GameViewController.h"
#import "GameScene.h"
#import "StartScene.h"
```

Then, in the `viewDidLoad` method, we just replace the code to create one scene class with another:

```
// Create and configure the scene.
GameScene *scene =
┬á┬á┬á┬á [GameScene sceneWithSize:self.view.frame.size levelNumber:1];
SKScene *┬áscene =┬á[StartScene sceneWithSize:skView.bounds.size];
```

Now give it a whirl! Launch the app, and youÔÇÖll be greeted by the start scene. Touch the screen, play the game, die a lot, and youÔÇÖll get to the game-over scene. Wait a few seconds, and youÔÇÖre back to the start screen, as shown in [Figure 17-17](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#Fig17).

![9781484202005_Fig17-17.jpg](images/9781484202005_Fig17-17.jpg)

[Figure 17-17](#9781484202005_Ch17.xhtml#_Fig17). Finally, we made it to the start screen!

A Sound Is Worth a Thousand Pictures

WeÔÇÖve been working on a video game, and video games are known for being noisy, but ours is completely silent! Fortunately, Sprite Kit contains audio playback code thatÔÇÖs extremely easy to use. In the *17 ÔÇô Sound Effects* folder in the source code for this chapter, youÔÇÖll find the prepared audio files: *enemyHit.wav*, *gameOver.wav*, *gameStart.wav*, *playerHit.wav*, and *shoot.wav*. Drag all of them into XcodeÔÇÖs Project Navigator.

**Note**┬á┬áThese sound effects were created using the excellent, open source CFXR application (available from `https://github.com/nevyn/cfxr`). If you need quirky little sound effects, CFXR is hard to beat!

Now weÔÇÖll bake in easy playback for each of these sound effects. Start with *BulletNode.m*, adding the bold code to the end of the `bulletFrom:toward:` method, just before the `return` line:

```
[bullet runAction:[SKAction playSoundFileNamed:@"shoot.wav"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á waitForCompletion:NO]];
```

Next, switch over *to EnemyNode.m*, adding these lines to the end of the `receiveAttacker:contact:` method:

```
[self runAction:[SKAction playSoundFileNamed:@"enemyHit.wav"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á waitForCompletion:NO]];
```

Now do something extremely similar in *PlayerNode.m*, adding these lines to the end of the `receiveAttacker:contact:` method:

```
[self runAction:[SKAction playSoundFileNamed:@"playerHit.wav"
┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á┬á waitForCompletion:NO]];
```

Those are enough in-game sounds to satisfy for the moment. Go ahead and run the game at this point to try them out. I think youÔÇÖll agree that the simple addition of particles and sounds gives the game a much better feel.
